infobox.key: Established in [1896](/wiki/1896_Major_League_Baseball_season "1896 Major League Baseball season")
infobox.value: * **Based in [Cleveland](/wiki/Cleveland "Cleveland") since [1900](/wiki/1900_Major_League_Baseball_season "1900 Major League Baseball season")** | |

are an American professional [baseball](/wiki/Baseball "Baseball") team based in [Cleveland](/wiki/Cleveland "Cleveland"). The Guardians compete in [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball") (MLB) as a member club of the [American League](/wiki/American_League "American League") (AL) [Central Division](/wiki/American_League_Central "American League Central"). Since [1994](/wiki/1994_in_baseball "1994 in baseball"), the team has played its home games at [Progressive Field](/wiki/Progressive_Field "Progressive Field") (originally known as Jacobs Field after the team's then-owner). Since their establishment as a Major League franchise in 1901, the team has won 13 Central Division titles, six [American League pennants](/wiki/List_of_American_League_pennant_winners "List of American League pennant winners"), and two [World Series](/wiki/World_Series "World Series") championships (in [1920](/wiki/1920_World_Series "1920 World Series") and [1948](/wiki/1948_World_Series "1948 World Series")). The team's World Series championship drought since 1948 is the [longest active](/wiki/List_of_Major_League_Baseball_franchise_postseason_droughts#Longest_current_World_Series_championship_drought "List of Major League Baseball franchise postseason droughts") among all 30 current Major League teams.[[2]](#cite_note-3)[[3]](#cite_note-:0-4) The team's name references the *Guardians of Traffic*, eight monolithic 1932 [Art Deco](/wiki/Art_Deco "Art Deco") sculptures by [Henry Hering](/wiki/Henry_Hering "Henry Hering") on the city's [Hope Memorial Bridge](/wiki/Hope_Memorial_Bridge "Hope Memorial Bridge"),[[4]](#cite_note-5) which is adjacent to Progressive Field.[[5]](#cite_note-ClevelandGuardians-6)[[6]](#cite_note-7) The team's mascot is named "Slider".[[7]](#cite_note-8) The team's [spring training](/wiki/Spring_training "Spring training") facility is at [Goodyear Ballpark](/wiki/Goodyear_Ballpark "Goodyear Ballpark") in [Goodyear, Arizona](/wiki/Goodyear,_Arizona "Goodyear, Arizona").[[8]](#cite_note-9)

(also known as the **Columbus Senators**), a minor league team based in [Columbus, Ohio](/wiki/Columbus,_Ohio "Columbus, Ohio") that played in the [Western League](/wiki/Western_League_(1885%E2%80%931899) "Western League (1885–1899)"). The team relocated to [Grand Rapids, Michigan](/wiki/Grand_Rapids,_Michigan "Grand Rapids, Michigan") midseason in 1899, where they were known as the **Grand Rapids Prodigals**.[[9]](#cite_note-IndyJournal-10)[[10]](#cite_note-prodigal-11) In 1900, the franchise moved to Cleveland and was called the **Cleveland Lake Shores**.[[11]](#cite_note-12) The Western League itself was renamed the American League prior to the 1900 season while continuing its minor league status. When the American League declared itself a major league in 1901, Cleveland was one of its eight charter franchises. Originally called the **Cleveland Bluebirds** or **Blues**, the team was also unofficially called the **Cleveland Broncos** in 1902. Beginning in 1903, the team was named the **Cleveland Napoleons** or **Naps**, after team captain and manager [Nap Lajoie](/wiki/Nap_Lajoie "Nap Lajoie").

The **Cleveland Guardians** are an American professional [baseball](/wiki/Baseball "Baseball") team based in [Cleveland](/wiki/Cleveland "Cleveland"). The Guardians compete in [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball") (MLB) as a member club of the [American League](/wiki/American_League "American League") (AL) [Central Division](/wiki/American_League_Central "American League Central"). Since [1994](/wiki/1994_in_baseball "1994 in baseball"), the team has played its home games at [Progressive Field](/wiki/Progressive_Field "Progressive Field") (originally known as Jacobs Field after the team's then-owner). Since their establishment as a Major League franchise in 1901, the team has won 13 Central Division titles, six [American League pennants](/wiki/List_of_American_League_pennant_winners "List of American League pennant winners"), and two [World Series](/wiki/World_Series "World Series") championships (in [1920](/wiki/1920_World_Series "1920 World Series") and [1948](/wiki/1948_World_Series "1948 World Series")). The team's World Series championship drought since 1948 is the [longest active](/wiki/List_of_Major_League_Baseball_franchise_postseason_droughts#Longest_current_World_Series_championship_drought "List of Major League Baseball franchise postseason droughts") among all 30 current Major League teams.[[2]](#cite_note-3)[[3]](#cite_note-:0-4) The team's name references the *Guardians of Traffic*, eight monolithic 1932 [Art Deco](/wiki/Art_Deco "Art Deco") sculptures by [Henry Hering](/wiki/Henry_Hering "Henry Hering") on the city's [Hope Memorial Bridge](/wiki/Hope_Memorial_Bridge "Hope Memorial Bridge"),[[4]](#cite_note-5) which is adjacent to Progressive Field.[[5]](#cite_note-ClevelandGuardians-6)[[6]](#cite_note-7) The team's mascot is named "Slider".[[7]](#cite_note-8) The team's [spring training](/wiki/Spring_training "Spring training") facility is at [Goodyear Ballpark](/wiki/Goodyear_Ballpark "Goodyear Ballpark") in [Goodyear, Arizona](/wiki/Goodyear,_Arizona "Goodyear, Arizona").[[8]](#cite_note-9)

The franchise originated in 1896 as the **Columbus Buckeyes** (also known as the **Columbus Senators**), a minor league team based in [Columbus, Ohio](/wiki/Columbus,_Ohio "Columbus, Ohio") that played in the [Western League](/wiki/Western_League_(1885%E2%80%931899) "Western League (1885–1899)"). The team relocated to [Grand Rapids, Michigan](/wiki/Grand_Rapids,_Michigan "Grand Rapids, Michigan") midseason in 1899, where they were known as the **Grand Rapids Prodigals**.[[9]](#cite_note-IndyJournal-10)[[10]](#cite_note-prodigal-11) In 1900, the franchise moved to Cleveland and was called the **Cleveland Lake Shores**.[[11]](#cite_note-12) The Western League itself was renamed the American League prior to the 1900 season while continuing its minor league status. When the American League declared itself a major league in 1901, Cleveland was one of its eight charter franchises. Originally called the **Cleveland Bluebirds** or **Blues**, the team was also unofficially called the **Cleveland Broncos** in 1902. Beginning in 1903, the team was named the **Cleveland Napoleons** or **Naps**, after team captain and manager [Nap Lajoie](/wiki/Nap_Lajoie "Nap Lajoie").

Lajoie left after the [1914 season](/wiki/1914_Cleveland_Naps_season "1914 Cleveland Naps season"), and club owner [Charles Somers](/wiki/Charles_Somers "Charles Somers") requested that baseball writers choose a new name. They chose the name **Cleveland Indians**. That name stuck and remained in use for more than a century. Common [nicknames](/wiki/List_of_baseball_nicknames "List of baseball nicknames") for the Indians were "the Tribe" and "the Wahoos", the latter referencing their longtime logo, [Chief Wahoo](/wiki/Chief_Wahoo "Chief Wahoo"). After the Indians name [came under criticism](/wiki/Cleveland_Indians_name_and_logo_controversy "Cleveland Indians name and logo controversy") as part of the [Native American mascot controversy](/wiki/Native_American_mascot_controversy "Native American mascot controversy"), the team adopted the current name, Guardians, starting with the [2022 season](/wiki/2022_Cleveland_Guardians_season "2022 Cleveland Guardians season").

From August 24 to September 14, 2017, the team won 22 consecutive games, the [longest winning streak](/wiki/List_of_Major_League_Baseball_longest_winning_streaks "List of Major League Baseball longest winning streaks") in American League history and the second longest winning streak in MLB history.

As of the end of the 2025 season[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Cleveland_Guardians&action=edit), the franchise's overall major league record is 9,940–9,443–91 (.513).[[12]](#cite_note-13)

## Early Cleveland baseball teams

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cleveland_Guardians&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early Cleveland baseball teams")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/Guardian_of_Traffic_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Guardian_of_Traffic_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Guardian_of_Traffic_(cropped).jpg)

The team is named after the eight *Guardians of Traffic* statues displayed on the [Hope Memorial Bridge](/wiki/Hope_Memorial_Bridge "Hope Memorial Bridge") next to their home field.

According to one historian of baseball, "in 1857, baseball games were a daily spectacle in Cleveland's Public Squares. City authorities tried to find an ordinance forbidding it; to the joy of the crowd, they were unsuccessful."[[13]](#cite_note-14)

### 1865–1872: Forest Citys of Cleveland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cleveland_Guardians&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 1865–1872: Forest Citys of Cleveland")]

From 1865 to 1868 Forest Citys was an amateur ball club. During the [1869 season](/wiki/1869_in_baseball "1869 in baseball"), Cleveland was among several cities that established professional baseball teams following the success of the 1869 [Cincinnati Red Stockings](/wiki/Cincinnati_Red_Stockings "Cincinnati Red Stockings"), the first fully professional team.[[14]](#cite_note-15)[[15]](#cite_note-16) In the newspapers before and after 1870, the team was often called the [Forest Citys](/wiki/Cleveland_Forest_Citys "Cleveland Forest Citys"), in the same generic way that the team from Chicago was sometimes called The Chicagos. A professional baseball team has played in Cleveland for all but a few years since then.

In 1871 the Forest Citys joined the new [National Association of Professional Base Ball Players](/wiki/National_Association_of_Professional_Base_Ball_Players "National Association of Professional Base Ball Players") (NA), the first professional league. Ultimately, two of the league's western clubs went out of business during the first season and the [Chicago Fire](/wiki/1871_Great_Chicago_Fire "1871 Great Chicago Fire") left that city's [White Stockings](/wiki/Chicago_Cubs "Chicago Cubs") impoverished, unable to field a team again until 1874. Cleveland was thus the NA's westernmost outpost in 1872, the year the club folded. Cleveland played its full schedule to July 19 followed by two games versus Boston in mid-August and disbanded at the end of the season.[[16]](#cite_note-17)

### 1879–1884: Cleveland Forest Citys and Blues

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cleveland_Guardians&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1879–1884: Cleveland Forest Citys and Blues")]

In 1876, the [National League](/wiki/National_League_(baseball) "National League (baseball)") (NL) supplanted the NA as the major professional league. Cleveland was not among its charter members, but by 1879 the league was looking for new entries and the city gained an NL team. A new Cleveland Forest Citys were recreated, but by 1882 were known as the [Cleveland Blues](/wiki/Cleveland_Blues_(NL) "Cleveland Blues (NL)"), because the National League required distinct colors for that season. The Blues had mediocre records for six seasons and were ruined by a trade war with the [Union Association](/wiki/Union_Association "Union Association") (UA) in 1884, when its three best players ([Fred Dunlap](/wiki/Fred_Dunlap "Fred Dunlap"), [Jack Glasscock](/wiki/Jack_Glasscock "Jack Glasscock"), and [Jim McCormick](/wiki/Jim_McCormick_(pitcher) "Jim McCormick (pitcher)")) jumped to the UA after being offered higher salaries. The Cleveland Blues merged with the St. Louis Maroons UA team in 1885.

### 1887–1899: Cleveland Spiders (nicknamed "Blues")

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cleveland_Guardians&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1887–1899: Cleveland Spiders (nicknamed \"Blues\")")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/T205_Cy_Young.jpg/250px-T205_Cy_Young.jpg)](/wiki/File:T205_Cy_Young.jpg)

[Cy Young](/wiki/Cy_Young "Cy Young") on a 1911 baseball card

Cleveland went without major league baseball for two seasons until gaining a team in the [American Association](/wiki/American_Association_(19th_century) "American Association (19th century)") (AA) in 1887. After the AA's [Pittsburgh Alleghenys](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Alleghenys "Pittsburgh Alleghenys") jumped to the NL, Cleveland followed suit in 1889, as the AA began to crumble. The Cleveland ball club, called the [Spiders](/wiki/Cleveland_Spiders "Cleveland Spiders") (supposedly inspired by their "skinny and spindly" players), slowly became a power in the league.[[17]](#cite_note-18) In 1891, the Spiders moved into [League Park](/wiki/League_Park "League Park"), which would serve as the home of Cleveland professional baseball for the next 55 years. Led by native Ohioan [Cy Young](/wiki/Cy_Young "Cy Young"), the Spiders became a contender in the mid-1890s, playing in the [Temple Cup](/wiki/Temple_Cup "Temple Cup") Series (that era's World Series) twice and winning it in 1895. The team began to fade after this success and was dealt a severe blow under the ownership of the [Robison brothers](/wiki/Robison_Field "Robison Field").

Prior to the [1899](/wiki/1899_in_baseball "1899 in baseball") season, Frank Robison, the Spiders' owner, bought the [St. Louis Browns](/wiki/St._Louis_Browns_(NL) "St. Louis Browns (NL)"), thus owning two clubs at the same time. The Browns were renamed the "Perfectos" and restocked with Cleveland talent. Just weeks before the season opener, most of the better Spiders were transferred to St. Louis, including three future Hall of Famers: Cy Young, [Jesse Burkett](/wiki/Jesse_Burkett "Jesse Burkett") and [Bobby Wallace](/wiki/Bobby_Wallace_(baseball) "Bobby Wallace (baseball)").[[18]](#cite_note-perfectos-19) The roster maneuvers failed to create a powerhouse Perfectos team, as St. Louis finished fifth in both 1899 and [1900](/wiki/1900_in_baseball "1900 in baseball"). The Spiders were left with essentially a minor league lineup and began to lose games at a record pace. Drawing almost no fans at home, they ended up playing most of their season on the road, and became known as "The Wanderers".[[19]](#cite_note-20) The team ended the season in 12th place, 84 games out of first place, with an all-time worst record of 20–134 (.130 winning percentage).[[20]](#cite_note-21) Following the 1899 season, the National League disbanded four teams, including the Spiders franchise. The disastrous 1899 season would actually be a step toward a new future for Cleveland fans the next year.

### 1890: Cleveland Infants (nickname "Babes")

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cleveland_Guardians&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 1890: Cleveland Infants (nickname \"Babes\")")]

The Cleveland Infants competed in the [Players' League](/wiki/Players%27_League "Players' League"), which was well-attended in some cities, but club owners lacked the confidence to continue beyond the one season. The Cleveland Infants finished with 55 wins and 75 losses, playing their home games at [Brotherhood Park](/wiki/Brotherhood_Park "Brotherhood Park").[[21]](#cite_note-22)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cleveland_Guardians&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of the Cleveland Guardians](/wiki/History_of_the_Cleveland_Guardians "History of the Cleveland Guardians")

### 1896–1935: Columbus, Grand Rapids, and the early Cleveland years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cleveland_Guardians&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 1896–1935: Columbus, Grand Rapids, and the early Cleveland years")]

The origins of the team date back to 1896, when the team was founded as the **Columbus Buckeyes**, a team based in [Columbus, Ohio](/wiki/Columbus,_Ohio "Columbus, Ohio") and competing in the [Western League](/wiki/Western_League_(1885%E2%80%931899) "Western League (1885–1899)"). The club was owned by [Tom Loftus](/wiki/Tom_Loftus "Tom Loftus"), a close friend of both league president [Ban Johnson](/wiki/Ban_Johnson "Ban Johnson") and [Charlie Comiskey](/wiki/Charlie_Comiskey "Charlie Comiskey"), owner of the league's [St. Paul Saints](/wiki/St._Paul_Saints_(1884%E2%80%931900) "St. Paul Saints (1884–1900)").[[22]](#cite_note-ALEvolution-23) In July 1899, the Western League franchise swapped cities midseason with the Grand Rapids franchise of the Interstate League, becoming the **Grand Rapids Prodigals** while remaining in the Western League.[[9]](#cite_note-IndyJournal-10)[[10]](#cite_note-prodigal-11)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/1899_Columbus_Senators_Grand_Rapids_Furniture_Makers.jpg/250px-1899_Columbus_Senators_Grand_Rapids_Furniture_Makers.jpg)](/wiki/File:1899_Columbus_Senators_Grand_Rapids_Furniture_Makers.jpg)

The 1899 Columbus/Grand Rapids team. Hall of Famer [Rube Waddell](/wiki/Rube_Waddell "Rube Waddell") is back row, second from the left.

In 1900, the team moved to Cleveland and was named the Cleveland Lake Shores, coinciding with Ban Johnson's change of the league name from the Western League to the American League.[[23]](#cite_note-CLEownerhist-24)[[24]](#cite_note-plaindealer1900-25) In 1900, the American League was still considered a minor league. In 1901, the team was called the Cleveland Bluebirds or Blues when the American League broke with the National Agreement and declared itself a competing Major League. The Cleveland franchise was among its eight charter members and is one of four teams that remain in its original city, along with [Boston](/wiki/Boston_Red_Sox "Boston Red Sox"), [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago_White_Sox "Chicago White Sox"), and [Detroit](/wiki/Detroit_Tigers "Detroit Tigers").

The new team was owned by coal magnate [Charles Somers](/wiki/Charles_Somers "Charles Somers") and tailor Jack Kilfoyl. Somers, a wealthy industrialist and also co-owner of the [Boston Americans](/wiki/Boston_Americans "Boston Americans"), lent money to other team owners, including [Connie Mack](/wiki/Connie_Mack "Connie Mack")'s [Philadelphia Athletics](/wiki/Philadelphia_Athletics "Philadelphia Athletics"), to keep them and the new league afloat. Players did not think the name "Bluebirds" was suitable for a baseball team.[[25]](#cite_note-TBT-26) Writers frequently shortened it to Cleveland Blues due to the players' all-blue uniforms,[[26]](#cite_note-27) but the players did not like this unofficial name either.[[27]](#cite_note-28) The players themselves tried to change the name to Cleveland Bronchos in [1902](/wiki/1902_in_baseball "1902 in baseball"), but this name never caught on.[[25]](#cite_note-TBT-26)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/Nap_Lajoie_1913.jpg/250px-Nap_Lajoie_1913.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nap_Lajoie_1913.jpg)

[Nap Lajoie](/wiki/Nap_Lajoie "Nap Lajoie"), who won the 1903 American League Batting Championship with the Cleveland Naps, was the team's namesake from 1903 to 1915, and is an [MLB Hall of Famer](/wiki/MLB_Hall_of_Fame "MLB Hall of Fame").

Cleveland suffered from financial problems in their first two seasons. This led Somers to seriously consider moving to either [Pittsburgh](/wiki/Pittsburgh "Pittsburgh") or [Cincinnati](/wiki/Cincinnati "Cincinnati"). Relief came in 1902 as a result of the conflict between the National and American Leagues. In 1901, [Napoleon "Nap" Lajoie](/wiki/Nap_Lajoie "Nap Lajoie"), the [Philadelphia Phillies](/wiki/Philadelphia_Phillies "Philadelphia Phillies")' star second baseman, jumped to the A's after his contract was capped at $2,400 per year—one of the highest-profile players to jump to the upstart AL. The Phillies subsequently filed an injunction to force Lajoie's return, which was granted by the [Pennsylvania Supreme Court](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Supreme_Court "Pennsylvania Supreme Court"). The injunction appeared to doom any hopes of an early settlement between the warring leagues. However, a lawyer discovered that the injunction was only enforceable in the state of Pennsylvania.[[25]](#cite_note-TBT-26) Mack, partly to thank Somers for his past financial support, agreed to trade Lajoie to the then-moribund Blues, who offered $25,000 salary over three years.[[28]](#cite_note-29) Due to the injunction, however, Lajoie had to sit out any games played against the A's in Philadelphia.[[29]](#cite_note-30) Lajoie arrived in Cleveland on June 4 and was an immediate hit, drawing 10,000 fans to League Park. Soon afterward, he was named team captain, and in 1903 the team was called the Cleveland Napoleons or Naps after a newspaper conducted a write-in contest.[[25]](#cite_note-TBT-26)

Lajoie was named manager in [1905](/wiki/1905_Major_League_Baseball_season "1905 Major League Baseball season"), and the team's fortunes improved somewhat. They finished half a game short of the pennant in 1908.[[30]](#cite_note-1908AL-31) However, the success did not last and Lajoie resigned during the 1909 season as manager but remained on as a player.[[31]](#cite_note-32)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/1909_Cleveland_Naps.jpg/250px-1909_Cleveland_Naps.jpg)](/wiki/File:1909_Cleveland_Naps.jpg)

[1909 Cleveland Naps](/wiki/1909_Cleveland_Naps_season "1909 Cleveland Naps season")

After that, the team began to unravel, leading Kilfoyl to sell his share of the team to Somers. [Cy Young](/wiki/Cy_Young "Cy Young"), who returned to Cleveland in 1909, was ineffective for most of his three remaining years[[32]](#cite_note-33) and [Addie Joss](/wiki/Addie_Joss "Addie Joss") died from tubercular meningitis prior to the 1911 season.[[33]](#cite_note-34)

Despite a strong lineup anchored by the potent Lajoie and [Shoeless Joe Jackson](/wiki/Shoeless_Joe_Jackson "Shoeless Joe Jackson"), poor pitching kept the team below third place for most of the next decade. One reporter referred to the team as the Napkins, "because they fold up so easily". The team hit bottom in 1914 and 1915, finishing last place both years.[[34]](#cite_note-35)[[35]](#cite_note-36)

1915 brought significant changes to the team. Lajoie, nearly 40 years old, was no longer a top hitter in the league, batting only .258 in 1914. With Lajoie engaged in a feud with manager [Joe Birmingham](/wiki/Joe_Birmingham "Joe Birmingham"), the team sold Lajoie back to the A's.[[36]](#cite_note-37)

With Lajoie gone, the club needed a new name. Somers asked the local baseball writers to come up with a new name, and based on their input, the team was renamed the Cleveland Indians.[[37]](#cite_note-38)[[38]](#cite_note-39)[[39]](#cite_note-40) The name referred to the nickname "Indians" that was applied to the [Cleveland Spiders](/wiki/Cleveland_Spiders "Cleveland Spiders") baseball club during the time when [Louis Sockalexis](/wiki/Louis_Sockalexis "Louis Sockalexis"), a [Native American](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States"), played in Cleveland (1897–1899).[[40]](#cite_note-41)

At the same time, Somers' business ventures began to fail, leaving him deeply in debt. With the Indians playing poorly, attendance and revenue suffered.[[41]](#cite_note-Somers-42) Somers decided to trade Jackson midway through the 1915 season for two players and $31,500, one of the largest sums paid for a player at the time.[[42]](#cite_note-43)

By 1916, Somers was at the end of his tether, and sold the team to a syndicate headed by Chicago railroad contractor [James C. "Jack" Dunn](/wiki/Jim_Dunn_(baseball_owner) "Jim Dunn (baseball owner)").[[41]](#cite_note-Somers-42) Manager Lee Fohl, who had taken over in early 1915, acquired two minor league pitchers, [Stan Coveleski](/wiki/Stan_Coveleski "Stan Coveleski") and [Jim Bagby](/wiki/Jim_Bagby,_Sr. "Jim Bagby, Sr.") and traded for center fielder [Tris Speaker](/wiki/Tris_Speaker "Tris Speaker"), who was engaged in a salary dispute with the [Red Sox](/wiki/Boston_Red_Sox "Boston Red Sox").[[43]](#cite_note-44) All three would ultimately become key players in bringing a championship to Cleveland.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/1920_Cleveland_Indians_Pano.jpg/960px-1920_Cleveland_Indians_Pano.jpg)](/wiki/File:1920_Cleveland_Indians_Pano.jpg "The 1920 Indians, who won the first World Series Championship in team history")

The 1920 Indians, who won the first World Series Championship in team history

Speaker took over the reins as [player-manager](/wiki/Player-manager "Player-manager") in [1919](/wiki/1919_Major_League_Baseball_season "1919 Major League Baseball season"), and led the team to a championship in 1920. On August 16, 1920, the Indians were playing the [Yankees](/wiki/1920_New_York_Yankees_season "1920 New York Yankees season") at the [Polo Grounds](/wiki/Polo_Grounds "Polo Grounds") in New York. Shortstop [Ray Chapman](/wiki/Ray_Chapman "Ray Chapman"), who often crowded the plate, was batting against [Carl Mays](/wiki/Carl_Mays "Carl Mays"), who had an unusual underhand delivery. It was also late in the afternoon and the infield was completely shaded with the center field area (the batters' background) bathed in sunlight. As well, at the time, "part of every pitcher's job was to dirty up a new ball the moment it was thrown onto the field. By turns, they smeared it with dirt, licorice, tobacco juice; it was deliberately scuffed, sandpapered, scarred, cut, even spiked. The result was a misshapen, earth-colored ball that traveled through the air erratically, tended to soften in the later innings, and as it came over the plate, was very hard to see."[[44]](#cite_note-45)

In any case, Chapman did not move reflexively when Mays' pitch came his way. The pitch hit Chapman in the head, fracturing his skull. Chapman died the next day, becoming the only player to sustain a fatal injury from a pitched ball.[[45]](#cite_note-46) The Indians, who at the time were locked in a tight three-way pennant race with the Yankees and [White Sox](/wiki/1920_Chicago_White_Sox_season "1920 Chicago White Sox season"),[[46]](#cite_note-47) were not slowed down by the death of their teammate. Rookie [Joe Sewell](/wiki/Joe_Sewell "Joe Sewell") hit .329 after replacing Chapman in the lineup.[[47]](#cite_note-48)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/TrisSpeakerGoudeycard.jpg/250px-TrisSpeakerGoudeycard.jpg)](/wiki/File:TrisSpeakerGoudeycard.jpg)

[Tris Speaker](/wiki/Tris_Speaker "Tris Speaker") on a 1933 baseball card

In September 1920, the [Black Sox Scandal](/wiki/Black_Sox_Scandal "Black Sox Scandal") came to a boil. With just a few games left in the season, and Cleveland and Chicago neck-and-neck for first place at 94–54 and 95–56 respectively,[[48]](#cite_note-49)[[49]](#cite_note-50) the Chicago owner suspended eight players. The White Sox lost two of three in their final series, while Cleveland won four and lost two in their final two series. Cleveland finished two games ahead of Chicago and three games ahead of the Yankees to win its first pennant, led by Speaker's .388 hitting, Jim Bagby's 30 victories and solid performances from [Steve O'Neill](/wiki/Steve_O%27Neill "Steve O'Neill") and Stan Coveleski. Cleveland went on to defeat the [Brooklyn Robins](/wiki/1920_Brooklyn_Robins_season "1920 Brooklyn Robins season") 5–2 in the [World Series](/wiki/1920_World_Series "1920 World Series") for their first title, winning four games in a row after the Robins took a 2–1 Series lead. The Series included three memorable "firsts", all of them in Game 5 at Cleveland, and all by the home team. In the first inning, right fielder [Elmer Smith](/wiki/Elmer_Smith_(20th_century_outfielder) "Elmer Smith (20th century outfielder)") hit the first Series grand slam. In the fourth inning, [Jim Bagby](/wiki/Jim_Bagby,_Sr. "Jim Bagby, Sr.") hit the first Series home run by a pitcher. In the top of the fifth inning, second baseman [Bill Wambsganss](/wiki/Bill_Wambsganss "Bill Wambsganss") executed the first (and only, so far) unassisted triple play in World Series history, in fact, the only Series triple play of any kind.

The team would not reach the heights of 1920 again for 28 years. Speaker and Coveleski were aging and the Yankees were rising with a new weapon: [Babe Ruth](/wiki/Babe_Ruth "Babe Ruth") and the home run. They managed two second-place finishes but spent much of the decade in last place. In 1927 Dunn's widow, Mrs. George Pross (Dunn had died in 1922), sold the team to a syndicate headed by [Alva Bradley](/wiki/Alva_Bradley "Alva Bradley").

### 1936–1946: Bob Feller enters the show

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cleveland_Guardians&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 1936–1946: Bob Feller enters the show")]

The Indians were a middling team by the 1930s, finishing third or fourth most years. [1936](/wiki/1936_in_baseball "1936 in baseball") brought Cleveland a new superstar in 17-year-old pitcher [Bob Feller](/wiki/Bob_Feller "Bob Feller"), who came from [Iowa](/wiki/Iowa "Iowa") with a dominating [fastball](/wiki/Fastball "Fastball"). That season, Feller set a record with 17 strikeouts in a single game and went on to lead the league in strikeouts from 1938 to 1941.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/05/Bob_Feller_1953.jpg/250px-Bob_Feller_1953.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bob_Feller_1953.jpg)

[Bob Feller](/wiki/Bob_Feller "Bob Feller"); winner of the A.L. pitching [Triple Crown](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball_Triple_Crown "Major League Baseball Triple Crown") in 1940, member of the [1948 World Series](/wiki/1948_World_Series "1948 World Series") Championship team, the Indians all-time leader in wins and strikeouts, and an [MLB Hall of Famer](/wiki/MLB_Hall_of_Fame "MLB Hall of Fame")

On August 20, 1938, Indians catchers [Hank Helf](/wiki/Hank_Helf "Hank Helf") and [Frank Pytlak](/wiki/Frank_Pytlak "Frank Pytlak") set the "all-time altitude mark" by catching baseballs dropped from the 708-foot (216 m) [Terminal Tower](/wiki/Terminal_Tower "Terminal Tower").[[50]](#cite_note-51)

By [1940](/wiki/1940_Major_League_Baseball_season "1940 Major League Baseball season"), Feller, along with [Ken Keltner](/wiki/Ken_Keltner "Ken Keltner"), [Mel Harder](/wiki/Mel_Harder "Mel Harder") and [Lou Boudreau](/wiki/Lou_Boudreau "Lou Boudreau"), led the Indians to within one game of the pennant. However, the team was wracked with dissension, with some players (including Feller and Mel Harder) going so far as to request that Bradley fire manager [Ossie Vitt](/wiki/Ossie_Vitt "Ossie Vitt"). Reporters lampooned them as the Cleveland Crybabies.[[51]](#cite_note-52)[*[better source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Questionable_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] Feller, who had pitched a [no-hitter](/wiki/No-hitter "No-hitter") to open the season and won 27 games, lost the final game of the season to unknown pitcher Floyd Giebell of the [Detroit Tigers](/wiki/Detroit_Tigers "Detroit Tigers"). The [Tigers](/wiki/1940_Detroit_Tigers_season "1940 Detroit Tigers season") won the pennant and Giebell never won another major league game.[[52]](#cite_note-53)

Cleveland entered 1941 with a young team and a new manager; [Roger Peckinpaugh](/wiki/Roger_Peckinpaugh "Roger Peckinpaugh") had replaced the despised Vitt; but the team regressed, finishing in fourth. Cleveland would soon be depleted of two stars. [Hal Trosky](/wiki/Hal_Trosky "Hal Trosky") retired in 1941 due to migraine headaches[[53]](#cite_note-54) and Bob Feller enlisted in the [Navy](/wiki/United_States_Navy "United States Navy") two days after the [Attack on Pearl Harbor](/wiki/Attack_on_Pearl_Harbor "Attack on Pearl Harbor"). Starting third baseman [Ken Keltner](/wiki/Ken_Keltner "Ken Keltner") and outfielder [Ray Mack](/wiki/Ray_Mack "Ray Mack") were both drafted in 1945 taking two more starters out of the lineup.[[54]](#cite_note-55)

### 1946–1949: The Bill Veeck years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cleveland_Guardians&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 1946–1949: The Bill Veeck years")]

In [1946](/wiki/1946_in_baseball "1946 in baseball"), [Bill Veeck](/wiki/Bill_Veeck "Bill Veeck") formed an investment group that purchased the Cleveland Indians from Bradley's group for a reported $1.6 million.[[55]](#cite_note-56) Among the investors was [Bob Hope](/wiki/Bob_Hope "Bob Hope"), who had grown up in Cleveland, and former Tigers slugger, [Hank Greenberg](/wiki/Hank_Greenberg "Hank Greenberg").[[56]](#cite_note-57)
A former owner of a minor league franchise in Milwaukee, Veeck brought to Cleveland a gift for promotion. At one point, Veeck hired rubber-faced[[57]](#cite_note-58) [Max Patkin](/wiki/Max_Patkin "Max Patkin"), the "Clown Prince of Baseball" as a coach. Patkin's appearance in the coaching box was the sort of promotional stunt that delighted fans but infuriated the American League front office.

Recognizing that he had acquired a solid team, Veeck soon abandoned the aging, small and lightless League Park to take up full-time residence in massive [Cleveland Municipal Stadium](/wiki/Cleveland_Municipal_Stadium "Cleveland Municipal Stadium").[[58]](#cite_note-Borsvold-59) The Indians had briefly moved from League Park to Municipal Stadium in mid-1932, but moved back to League Park due to complaints about the cavernous environment. From 1937 onward, however, the Indians began playing an increasing number of games at Municipal, until by 1940 they played most of their home slate there.[[59]](#cite_note-60) League Park was mostly demolished in 1951, but has since been rebuilt as a recreational park.[[60]](#cite_note-61)

Making the most of the cavernous stadium, Veeck had a portable center field fence installed, which he could move in or out depending on how the distance favored the Indians against their opponents in a given series. The fence moved as much as 15 feet (5 m) between series opponents. Following the 1947 season, the American League countered with a rule change that fixed the distance of an outfield wall for the duration of a season. The massive stadium did, however, permit the Indians to set the then-record for the largest crowd to see a Major League baseball game. On October 10, 1948, Game 5 of the [World Series](/wiki/1948_World_Series "1948 World Series") against the [Boston Braves](/wiki/Boston_Braves_(baseball) "Boston Braves (baseball)") drew over 84,000. The record stood until the [Los Angeles Dodgers](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Dodgers "Los Angeles Dodgers") drew a crowd in excess of 92,500 to watch Game 5 of the [1959 World Series](/wiki/1959_World_Series "1959 World Series") at the [Los Angeles Memorial Coliseum](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Memorial_Coliseum "Los Angeles Memorial Coliseum") against the [Chicago White Sox](/wiki/Chicago_White_Sox "Chicago White Sox").

Under Veeck's leadership, one of Cleveland's most significant achievements was breaking the [color barrier](/wiki/Baseball_color_line "Baseball color line") in the [American League](/wiki/American_League "American League") by signing [Larry Doby](/wiki/Larry_Doby "Larry Doby"), formerly a player for the Negro league's [Newark Eagles](/wiki/Newark_Eagles "Newark Eagles") in [1947](/wiki/1947_in_baseball "1947 in baseball"), 11 weeks after [Jackie Robinson](/wiki/Jackie_Robinson "Jackie Robinson") signed with the [Dodgers](/wiki/Brooklyn_Dodgers "Brooklyn Dodgers").[[58]](#cite_note-Borsvold-59) Similar to Robinson, Doby battled racism on and off the field but posted a .301 batting average in 1948, his first full season. A power-hitting center fielder, Doby led the American League twice in homers.

In 1948, needing pitching for the stretch run of the pennant race, Veeck turned to the [Negro leagues](/wiki/Negro_leagues "Negro leagues") again and signed pitching great [Satchel Paige](/wiki/Satchel_Paige "Satchel Paige") amid much controversy.[[58]](#cite_note-Borsvold-59) Barred from Major League Baseball during his prime, Veeck's signing of the aging star in 1948 was viewed by many as another publicity stunt. At an official age of 42, Paige became the oldest rookie in Major League baseball history, and the first black pitcher. Paige ended the year with a 6–1 record with a 2.48 ERA, 45 strikeouts and two shutouts.[[61]](#cite_note-62)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/25/LouBoudreau1953bowman.jpg/250px-LouBoudreau1953bowman.jpg)](/wiki/File:LouBoudreau1953bowman.jpg)

[Lou Boudreau](/wiki/Lou_Boudreau "Lou Boudreau"), 1948 American League MVP

In [1948](/wiki/1948_Major_League_Baseball_season "1948 Major League Baseball season"), veterans Boudreau, Keltner, and [Joe Gordon](/wiki/Joe_Gordon "Joe Gordon") had career offensive seasons, while newcomers Doby and [Gene Bearden](/wiki/Gene_Bearden "Gene Bearden") also had standout seasons. The team went down to the wire with the [Boston Red Sox](/wiki/1948_Boston_Red_Sox_season "1948 Boston Red Sox season"), winning a one-game playoff, the first in American League history, to go to the [World Series](/wiki/1948_World_Series "1948 World Series"). In the series, the Indians defeated the [Boston Braves](/wiki/Atlanta_Braves#History "Atlanta Braves") four games to two for their first championship in 28 years. Boudreau won the [American League MVP Award](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball_Most_Valuable_Player_Award "Major League Baseball Most Valuable Player Award").

The Indians appeared in a film the following [year](/wiki/1949_in_film "1949 in film") titled *[The Kid From Cleveland](/wiki/The_Kid_From_Cleveland "The Kid From Cleveland")*, in which Veeck had an interest.[[58]](#cite_note-Borsvold-59) The film portrayed the team helping out a "troubled teenaged fan"[[62]](#cite_note-63) and featured many members of the Indians organization. However, filming during the season cost the players valuable rest days leading to fatigue towards the end of the season.[[58]](#cite_note-Borsvold-59) That season, Cleveland again contended before falling to third place. On September 23, 1949, Bill Veeck and the Indians buried their 1948 pennant in center field the day after they were mathematically eliminated from the pennant race.[[58]](#cite_note-Borsvold-59)

Later in 1949, Veeck's first wife (who had a half-stake in Veeck's share of the team) divorced him. With most of his money tied up in the Indians, Veeck was forced to sell the team[[63]](#cite_note-64) to a syndicate headed by insurance magnate Ellis Ryan.

### 1950–1959: Near misses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cleveland_Guardians&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: 1950–1959: Near misses")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/Al_Rosen_1953.jpg/250px-Al_Rosen_1953.jpg)](/wiki/File:Al_Rosen_1953.jpg)

[Al Rosen](/wiki/Al_Rosen "Al Rosen"), 1953 Most Valuable Player

In [1953](/wiki/1953_Major_League_Baseball_season "1953 Major League Baseball season"), [Al Rosen](/wiki/Al_Rosen "Al Rosen") was an All Star for the second year in a row, was named *The Sporting News* Major League Player of the Year, and won the [American League Most Valuable Player Award](/wiki/American_League_Most_Valuable_Player_Award "American League Most Valuable Player Award") in a unanimous vote playing for the Indians after leading the AL in runs, home runs, RBIs (for the second year in a row), and slugging percentage, and coming in second by one point in batting average.[[64]](#cite_note-65) Ryan was forced out in 1953 in favor of Myron Wilson, who in turn gave way to William Daley in [1956](/wiki/1956_Major_League_Baseball_season "1956 Major League Baseball season"). Despite this turnover in the ownership, a powerhouse team composed of Feller, Doby, [Minnie Miñoso](/wiki/Minnie_Mi%C3%B1oso "Minnie Miñoso"), [Luke Easter](/wiki/Luke_Easter_(baseball) "Luke Easter (baseball)"), [Bobby Ávila](/wiki/Bobby_%C3%81vila "Bobby Ávila"), [Al Rosen](/wiki/Al_Rosen "Al Rosen"), [Early Wynn](/wiki/Early_Wynn "Early Wynn"), [Bob Lemon](/wiki/Bob_Lemon "Bob Lemon"), and [Mike Garcia](/wiki/Mike_Garcia_(AL_pitcher) "Mike Garcia (AL pitcher)") continued to contend through the early 1950s. However, Cleveland only won a single pennant in the decade, in 1954, finishing second to the [New York Yankees](/wiki/1954_New_York_Yankees_season "1954 New York Yankees season") five times.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/Herb_Score_1955.JPG/250px-Herb_Score_1955.JPG)](/wiki/File:Herb_Score_1955.JPG)

[Herb Score](/wiki/Herb_Score "Herb Score") – who was the 1955 [American League Rookie of the Year](/wiki/American_League_Rookie_of_the_Year "American League Rookie of the Year"), a two-time A.L. All-Star, and after his playing career was a member of the Indians broadcast team for 34 seasons (1964–1997).

The winningest season in franchise history came in 1954, when the [Indians](/wiki/1954_Cleveland_Indians_season "1954 Cleveland Indians season") finished the season with a record of 111–43 (.721). That mark set an American League record for wins that stood for 44 years until the [Yankees](/wiki/1998_New_York_Yankees_season "1998 New York Yankees season") won 114 games in 1998 (a 162-game regular season record of 114-48/.704). The Indians' 1954 winning percentage of .721 is still an American League record. The Indians returned to the [World Series](/wiki/1954_World_Series "1954 World Series") to face the [New York Giants](/wiki/1954_New_York_Giants_(MLB)_season "1954 New York Giants (MLB) season"). The team could not bring home the title, however, ultimately being upset by the Giants in a sweep. The series was notable for [Willie Mays](/wiki/Willie_Mays "Willie Mays")' [over-the-shoulder catch](/wiki/The_Catch_(baseball) "The Catch (baseball)") off the bat of [Vic Wertz](/wiki/Vic_Wertz "Vic Wertz") in Game 1. Cleveland remained a talented team throughout the remainder of the decade, finishing in second place in 1959, [George Strickland](/wiki/George_Strickland_(baseball) "George Strickland (baseball)")'s last full year in the majors.

### 1960–1993: The 33-year slump

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cleveland_Guardians&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: 1960–1993: The 33-year slump")]

From 1960 to 1993, the Indians managed one third-place finish (in 1968) and six fourth-place finishes (in 1960, 1974, 1975, 1976, 1990, and 1992) but spent the rest of the time at or near the bottom of the standings, including four seasons with over 100 losses (1971, 1985, 1987, 1991).

#### Frank Lane becomes general manager

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cleveland_Guardians&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Frank Lane becomes general manager")]

The Indians hired general manager [Frank Lane](/wiki/Frank_Lane "Frank Lane"), known as "Trader" Lane, away from the [St. Louis Cardinals](/wiki/St._Louis_Cardinals "St. Louis Cardinals") in 1957. Lane over the years had gained a reputation as a GM who loved to make deals. With the White Sox, Lane had made over 100 trades involving over 400 players in seven years.[[65]](#cite_note-Cards-66) In a short stint in St. Louis, he traded away [Red Schoendienst](/wiki/Red_Schoendienst "Red Schoendienst") and [Harvey Haddix](/wiki/Harvey_Haddix "Harvey Haddix").[[65]](#cite_note-Cards-66) Lane summed up his philosophy when he said that the only deals he regretted were the ones that he did not make.[[66]](#cite_note-67)

One of Lane's early trades in Cleveland was to send [Roger Maris](/wiki/Roger_Maris "Roger Maris") to the [Kansas City Athletics](/wiki/History_of_the_Kansas_City_Athletics "History of the Kansas City Athletics") in the middle of 1958. Indians executive [Hank Greenberg](/wiki/Hank_Greenberg "Hank Greenberg") was not happy about the trade[[67]](#cite_note-Maris-68) and neither was Maris, who said that he could not stand Lane.[[67]](#cite_note-Maris-68) After Maris broke Babe Ruth's home run record, Lane defended himself by saying he still would have done the deal because Maris was unknown and he received good ballplayers in exchange.[[67]](#cite_note-Maris-68)

After the Maris trade, Lane acquired 25-year-old [Norm Cash](/wiki/Norm_Cash "Norm Cash") from the White Sox for [Minnie Miñoso](/wiki/Minnie_Mi%C3%B1oso "Minnie Miñoso") and then traded him to Detroit before he ever played a game for the Indians; Cash went on to hit over 350 home runs for the Tigers. The Indians received [Steve Demeter](/wiki/Steve_Demeter "Steve Demeter") in the deal, who had only five at-bats for Cleveland.[[68]](#cite_note-69)

#### Curse of Rocky Colavito

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cleveland_Guardians&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Curse of Rocky Colavito")]

See also: [Curse of Rocky Colavito](/wiki/Curse_of_Rocky_Colavito "Curse of Rocky Colavito")

In 1960, Lane made the trade that would define his tenure in Cleveland when he dealt slugging right fielder and fan favorite [Rocky Colavito](/wiki/Rocky_Colavito "Rocky Colavito") to the [Detroit Tigers](/wiki/Detroit_Tigers "Detroit Tigers") for [Harvey Kuenn](/wiki/Harvey_Kuenn "Harvey Kuenn") just before Opening Day in [1960](/wiki/1960_Major_League_Baseball_season "1960 Major League Baseball season").[[69]](#cite_note-Colavito-70)

It was a blockbuster trade that swapped the [1959](/wiki/1959_Major_League_Baseball_season "1959 Major League Baseball season") AL home run co-champion (Colavito) for the AL batting champion (Kuenn). After the trade, however, Colavito hit over 30 home runs four times and made three All-Star teams for Detroit and Kansas City before returning to Cleveland in [1965](/wiki/1965_Major_League_Baseball_season "1965 Major League Baseball season"). Kuenn, on the other hand, played only one season for the Indians before departing for [San Francisco](/wiki/San_Francisco_Giants "San Francisco Giants") in a trade for an aging [Johnny Antonelli](/wiki/Johnny_Antonelli "Johnny Antonelli") and [Willie Kirkland](/wiki/Willie_Kirkland "Willie Kirkland"). *[Akron Beacon Journal](/wiki/Akron_Beacon_Journal "Akron Beacon Journal")* columnist [Terry Pluto](/wiki/Terry_Pluto "Terry Pluto") documented the decades of woe that followed the trade in his book *The Curse of Rocky Colavito*.[[70]](#cite_note-71) Despite being attached to the curse, Colavito said that he never placed a curse on the Indians but that the trade was prompted by a salary dispute with Lane.[[71]](#cite_note-Curse-72)

Lane also engineered a unique trade of managers in mid-season 1960, sending [Joe Gordon](/wiki/Joe_Gordon "Joe Gordon") to the Tigers in exchange for [Jimmy Dykes](/wiki/Jimmy_Dykes "Jimmy Dykes"). Lane left the team in 1961, but ill-advised trades continued. In 1965, the Indians traded pitcher [Tommy John](/wiki/Tommy_John "Tommy John"), who would go on to win 288 games in his career, and 1966 Rookie of the Year [Tommy Agee](/wiki/Tommy_Agee "Tommy Agee") to the White Sox to get Colavito back.[[71]](#cite_note-Curse-72)

However, Indians' pitchers set numerous strikeout records. They led the league in K's every year from 1963 to 1968, and narrowly missed in 1969. The 1964 staff was the first to amass 1,100 strikeouts, and in 1968, they were the first to collect more strikeouts than hits allowed.

#### Move to the AL East division

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cleveland_Guardians&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Move to the AL East division")]

The 1970s were not much better, with the Indians trading away several future stars, including [Graig Nettles](/wiki/Graig_Nettles "Graig Nettles"), [Dennis Eckersley](/wiki/Dennis_Eckersley "Dennis Eckersley"), [Buddy Bell](/wiki/Buddy_Bell "Buddy Bell") and 1971 Rookie of the Year [Chris Chambliss](/wiki/Chris_Chambliss "Chris Chambliss"),[[72]](#cite_note-73) for a number of players who made no impact.[[73]](#cite_note-74)

Constant ownership changes did not help the Indians. In 1963, Daley's syndicate sold the team to a group headed by general manager [Gabe Paul](/wiki/Gabe_Paul "Gabe Paul").[[25]](#cite_note-TBT-26) Three years later, Paul sold the Indians to [Vernon Stouffer](/wiki/Vernon_Stouffer "Vernon Stouffer"),[[74]](#cite_note-75) of the [Stouffer Corporation](/wiki/Stouffer_Corporation "Stouffer Corporation") empire. Prior to Stouffer's purchase, the team was rumored to be relocated due to poor attendance. Despite the potential for a financially strong owner, Stouffer had some non-baseball related financial setbacks and, consequently, the team was cash-poor. In order to solve some financial problems, Stouffer had made an agreement to play a minimum of 30 home games in [New Orleans](/wiki/New_Orleans "New Orleans") with a view to a possible move there.[[75]](#cite_note-Mileti-76) After rejecting an offer from [George Steinbrenner](/wiki/George_Steinbrenner "George Steinbrenner") and former Indian [Al Rosen](/wiki/Al_Rosen "Al Rosen"), Stouffer sold the team in 1972 to a group led by [Cleveland Cavaliers](/wiki/Cleveland_Cavaliers "Cleveland Cavaliers") and [Cleveland Barons](/wiki/Cleveland_Barons_(1937%E2%80%931973) "Cleveland Barons (1937–1973)") owner [Nick Mileti](/wiki/Nick_Mileti "Nick Mileti").[[75]](#cite_note-Mileti-76) Steinbrenner went on to buy the New York Yankees in 1973.[[76]](#cite_note-77)

Only five years later, Mileti's group sold the team for $11 million to a syndicate headed by trucking magnate Steve O'Neill and including former general manager and owner Gabe Paul.[[77]](#cite_note-78) O'Neill's death in 1983 led to the team going on the market once more. O'Neill's nephew Patrick O'Neill did not find a buyer until real estate magnates [Richard E. and David H. Jacobs](/wiki/Richard_Jacobs_(businessman) "Richard Jacobs (businessman)") purchased the team in 1986.[[78]](#cite_note-79)

The team was unable to move out of last place, with losing seasons between 1969 and 1975. One highlight was the acquisition of [Gaylord Perry](/wiki/Gaylord_Perry "Gaylord Perry") in [1972](/wiki/1972_Major_League_Baseball_season "1972 Major League Baseball season"). The Indians traded fireballer ["Sudden Sam" McDowell](/wiki/Sam_McDowell "Sam McDowell") for Perry, who became the first Indian pitcher to win the [Cy Young Award](/wiki/Cy_Young_Award "Cy Young Award"). In [1975](/wiki/1975_Major_League_Baseball_season "1975 Major League Baseball season"), Cleveland broke another color barrier with the hiring of [Frank Robinson](/wiki/Frank_Robinson "Frank Robinson") as Major League Baseball's first African American manager. Robinson served as player-manager and provided a franchise highlight when he hit a pinch-hit home run on Opening Day. But the high-profile signing of [Wayne Garland](/wiki/Wayne_Garland "Wayne Garland"), a 20-game winner in [Baltimore](/wiki/Baltimore_Orioles "Baltimore Orioles"), proved to be a disaster after Garland suffered from shoulder problems and went 28–48 over five years.[[79]](#cite_note-80) The team failed to improve with Robinson as manager and he was fired in [1977](/wiki/1977_Major_League_Baseball_season "1977 Major League Baseball season"). In 1977, pitcher [Dennis Eckersley](/wiki/Dennis_Eckersley "Dennis Eckersley") threw a no-hitter against the [California Angels](/wiki/1977_California_Angels_season "1977 California Angels season"). The next season, he was traded to the [Boston Red Sox](/wiki/Boston_Red_Sox "Boston Red Sox") where he won 20 games in 1978 and another 17 in 1979.

The 1970s also featured the infamous [Ten Cent Beer Night](/wiki/Ten_Cent_Beer_Night "Ten Cent Beer Night") at Cleveland Municipal Stadium. The ill-conceived promotion at a 1974 game against the [Texas Rangers](/wiki/1974_Texas_Rangers_season "1974 Texas Rangers season") ended in a riot by fans and a forfeit by the Indians.[[80]](#cite_note-81)

There were more bright spots in the 1980s. In May 1981, [Len Barker](/wiki/Len_Barker "Len Barker") threw a [perfect game](/wiki/Perfect_game_(baseball) "Perfect game (baseball)") against the [Toronto Blue Jays](/wiki/1981_Toronto_Blue_Jays_season "1981 Toronto Blue Jays season"), joining Addie Joss as the only other Indian pitcher to do so.[[81]](#cite_note-Barker-82) ["Super Joe" Charboneau](/wiki/Joe_Charboneau "Joe Charboneau") won the [American League Rookie of the Year](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball_Rookie_of_the_Year_Award "Major League Baseball Rookie of the Year Award") award. Charboneau was out of baseball by 1983 due to back injuries[[82]](#cite_note-83) and Barker, who was also hampered by injuries, never became a consistently dominant starting pitcher.[[81]](#cite_note-Barker-82)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2e/Frank_Robinson_1975_%281%29.jpeg/250px-Frank_Robinson_1975_%281%29.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Frank_Robinson_1975_(1).jpeg)

In 1975, [Frank Robinson](/wiki/Frank_Robinson "Frank Robinson") became the first [African-American](/wiki/African-American "African-American") manager in MLB history.

Eventually, the Indians traded Barker to the [Atlanta Braves](/wiki/Atlanta_Braves "Atlanta Braves") for [Brett Butler](/wiki/Brett_Butler_(baseball) "Brett Butler (baseball)") and [Brook Jacoby](/wiki/Brook_Jacoby "Brook Jacoby"),[[81]](#cite_note-Barker-82) who became mainstays of the team for the remainder of the decade. Butler and Jacoby were joined by [Joe Carter](/wiki/Joe_Carter "Joe Carter"), [Mel Hall](/wiki/Mel_Hall "Mel Hall"), [Julio Franco](/wiki/Julio_Franco "Julio Franco") and [Cory Snyder](/wiki/Cory_Snyder "Cory Snyder"), bringing new hope to fans in the late 1980s.[[83]](#cite_note-84)

Cleveland's struggles over the 30-year span were highlighted in the 1989 film *[Major League](/wiki/Major_League_(film) "Major League (film)")*, which comically depicted a hapless Cleveland ball club going from worst to first by the end of the film.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/Slider_5-28-12.jpg/250px-Slider_5-28-12.jpg)](/wiki/File:Slider_5-28-12.jpg)

Slider, the team mascot since 1990

Throughout the 1980s, the Indians' owners had pushed for a new stadium. Cleveland Stadium had been a symbol of the Indians' glory years in the 1940s and 1950s.[[84]](#cite_note-85) However, during the lean years even crowds of 40,000 were swallowed up by the cavernous environment. The old stadium was not aging gracefully; chunks of concrete were falling off in sections and the old wooden pilings were petrifying.[[85]](#cite_note-86) In 1984, a proposal for a $150 million domed stadium was defeated in a referendum 2–1.[[86]](#cite_note-87)

Finally, in May 1990, [Cuyahoga County](/wiki/Cuyahoga_County,_Ohio "Cuyahoga County, Ohio") voters passed an [excise tax](/wiki/Pigovian_tax "Pigovian tax") on sales of alcohol and cigarettes in the county. The tax proceeds were to be used for financing the construction of the [Gateway Sports and Entertainment Complex](/wiki/Gateway_Sports_and_Entertainment_Complex "Gateway Sports and Entertainment Complex"), which would include [Jacobs Field](/wiki/Jacobs_Field "Jacobs Field") for the Indians and [Gund Arena](/wiki/Gund_Arena "Gund Arena") for the [Cleveland Cavs](/wiki/Cleveland_Cavs "Cleveland Cavs") basketball team.[[87]](#cite_note-88)

The team's fortunes started to turn in [1989](/wiki/1989_in_baseball "1989 in baseball"), ironically with a very unpopular trade. The team sent power-hitting outfielder [Joe Carter](/wiki/Joe_Carter "Joe Carter") to the [San Diego Padres](/wiki/San_Diego_Padres "San Diego Padres") for two unproven players, [Sandy Alomar Jr.](/wiki/Sandy_Alomar_Jr. "Sandy Alomar Jr.") and [Carlos Baerga](/wiki/Carlos_Baerga "Carlos Baerga"). Alomar made an immediate impact, not only being elected to the [All-Star team](/wiki/1990_Major_League_Baseball_All-Star_Game "1990 Major League Baseball All-Star Game") but also winning Cleveland's fourth [Rookie of the Year](/wiki/MLB_Rookie_of_the_Year_Award "MLB Rookie of the Year Award") award and a [Gold Glove](/wiki/Gold_Glove_Award "Gold Glove Award"). Baerga became a three-time All-Star with consistent offensive production.

Indians general manager [John Hart](/wiki/John_Hart_(baseball) "John Hart (baseball)") made a number of moves that finally brought success to the team. In [1991](/wiki/1991_Major_League_Baseball_season "1991 Major League Baseball season"), he hired former Indian [Mike Hargrove](/wiki/Mike_Hargrove "Mike Hargrove") to manage and traded catcher [Eddie Taubensee](/wiki/Eddie_Taubensee "Eddie Taubensee") to the [Houston Astros](/wiki/Houston_Astros "Houston Astros") who, with a surplus of outfielders, were willing to part with [Kenny Lofton](/wiki/Kenny_Lofton "Kenny Lofton"). Lofton finished second in AL Rookie of the Year balloting with a .285 average and 66 [stolen bases](/wiki/Stolen_base "Stolen base").

The Indians were named "Organization of the Year" by *Baseball America*[[88]](#cite_note-89) in 1992, in response to the appearance of offensive bright spots and an improving [farm system](/wiki/Farm_team "Farm team").

The team suffered a tragedy during spring training of [1993](/wiki/1993_Major_League_Baseball_season "1993 Major League Baseball season"), when a boat carrying pitchers [Steve Olin](/wiki/Steve_Olin "Steve Olin"), [Tim Crews](/wiki/Tim_Crews "Tim Crews"), and [Bob Ojeda](/wiki/Bob_Ojeda "Bob Ojeda") crashed into a pier. Olin and Crews were killed, and Ojeda was seriously injured. (Ojeda missed most of the season, and retired the following year).[[89]](#cite_note-90)

By the end of the 1993 season, the team was in transition, leaving Cleveland Stadium and fielding a talented nucleus of young players. Many of those players came from the Indians' new [AAA](/wiki/AAA_(baseball) "AAA (baseball)") farm team, the [Charlotte Knights](/wiki/Charlotte_Knights "Charlotte Knights"), who won the [International League](/wiki/International_League "International League") title that year.

### 1994–2001: New beginnings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cleveland_Guardians&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: 1994–2001: New beginnings")]

#### 1994: Jacobs Field opens

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cleveland_Guardians&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: 1994: Jacobs Field opens")]

Main article: [1994 Cleveland Indians season](/wiki/1994_Cleveland_Indians_season "1994 Cleveland Indians season")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/Jacobs_Field_main_sign.JPG/250px-Jacobs_Field_main_sign.JPG)](/wiki/File:Jacobs_Field_main_sign.JPG)

[Jacobs Field](/wiki/Jacobs_Field "Jacobs Field") sign pictured in 2006

Indians General Manager [John Hart](/wiki/John_Hart_(baseball) "John Hart (baseball)") and team owner [Richard E. Jacobs](/wiki/Richard_Jacobs_(businessman) "Richard Jacobs (businessman)") managed to turn the team's fortunes around. The Indians opened [Jacobs Field](/wiki/Jacobs_Field "Jacobs Field") in 1994 with the aim of improving on the prior season's sixth-place finish. The Indians were only one game behind the division-leading [Chicago White Sox](/wiki/1994_Chicago_White_Sox_season "1994 Chicago White Sox season") on August 12 when a [players strike](/wiki/1994%E2%80%9395_Major_League_Baseball_strike "1994–95 Major League Baseball strike") wiped out the rest of the season.

#### 1995–1996: First AL pennant since 1954

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cleveland_Guardians&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: 1995–1996: First AL pennant since 1954")]

Having contended for the division in the aborted 1994 season, Cleveland sprinted to a 100–44 record (the season was shortened by 18 games due to player/owner negotiations) in 1995, winning its first-ever divisional title. Veterans [Dennis Martínez](/wiki/Dennis_Mart%C3%ADnez "Dennis Martínez"), [Orel Hershiser](/wiki/Orel_Hershiser "Orel Hershiser") and [Eddie Murray](/wiki/Eddie_Murray "Eddie Murray") combined with a young core of players including [Omar Vizquel](/wiki/Omar_Vizquel "Omar Vizquel"), [Albert Belle](/wiki/Albert_Belle "Albert Belle"), [Jim Thome](/wiki/Jim_Thome "Jim Thome"), [Manny Ramírez](/wiki/Manny_Ram%C3%ADrez "Manny Ramírez"), [Kenny Lofton](/wiki/Kenny_Lofton "Kenny Lofton") and [Charles Nagy](/wiki/Charles_Nagy "Charles Nagy") to lead the league in team batting average as well as team ERA.

After defeating the [Boston Red Sox](/wiki/1995_Boston_Red_Sox_season "1995 Boston Red Sox season") in the [Division Series](/wiki/1995_American_League_Division_Series "1995 American League Division Series") and the [Seattle Mariners](/wiki/1995_Seattle_Mariners_season "1995 Seattle Mariners season") in the [ALCS](/wiki/1995_American_League_Championship_Series "1995 American League Championship Series"), Cleveland clinched the American League pennant and a [World Series](/wiki/World_Series "World Series") berth, for the first time since 1954. The [World Series](/wiki/1995_World_Series "1995 World Series") ended in disappointment, however: the Indians fell in six games to the [Atlanta Braves](/wiki/1995_Atlanta_Braves_season "1995 Atlanta Braves season").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8b/Kenny_Lofton_1996.jpg/250px-Kenny_Lofton_1996.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kenny_Lofton_1996.jpg)

[Kenny Lofton](/wiki/Kenny_Lofton "Kenny Lofton") in 1996

Tickets for every Indians home game sold out several months before opening day in 1996.[[90]](#cite_note-Tickets-91) The Indians repeated as AL Central champions but lost to the [wild card](/wiki/Wild_card_(sports) "Wild card (sports)") [Baltimore Orioles](/wiki/1996_Baltimore_Orioles_season "1996 Baltimore Orioles season") in the [Division Series](/wiki/1996_American_League_Division_Series "1996 American League Division Series").

#### 1997: Two outs away

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cleveland_Guardians&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: 1997: Two outs away")]

Main article: [1997 Cleveland Indians season](/wiki/1997_Cleveland_Indians_season "1997 Cleveland Indians season")

In 1997, Cleveland started slow but finished with an 86–75 record. Taking their third consecutive AL Central title, the Indians defeated the [New York Yankees](/wiki/1997_New_York_Yankees_season "1997 New York Yankees season") in the [Division Series](/wiki/1997_American_League_Division_Series "1997 American League Division Series"), 3–2. After defeating the [Baltimore Orioles](/wiki/1997_Baltimore_Orioles_season "1997 Baltimore Orioles season") in the [ALCS](/wiki/1997_American_League_Championship_Series "1997 American League Championship Series"), Cleveland went on to face the [Florida Marlins](/wiki/1997_Florida_Marlins_season "1997 Florida Marlins season") in the [World Series](/wiki/1997_World_Series "1997 World Series") that featured the coldest game in World Series history. With the series tied after Game 6, the Indians went into the ninth inning of Game Seven with a 2–1 lead, but closer [José Mesa](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Mesa "José Mesa") allowed the Marlins to tie the game. In the eleventh inning, [Édgar Rentería](/wiki/%C3%89dgar_Renter%C3%ADa "Édgar Rentería") drove in the winning run giving the Marlins their first championship. Cleveland became the first team to lose the World Series after carrying the lead into the ninth inning of the seventh game.[[3]](#cite_note-:0-4)

#### 1998–2001

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cleveland_Guardians&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: 1998–2001")]

In [1998](/wiki/1998_Cleveland_Indians_season "1998 Cleveland Indians season"), the Indians made the postseason for the fourth straight year. After defeating the wild-card [Boston Red Sox](/wiki/1998_Boston_Red_Sox_season "1998 Boston Red Sox season") 3–1 in the [Division Series](/wiki/1998_American_League_Division_Series "1998 American League Division Series"), Cleveland lost the [1998 ALCS](/wiki/1998_American_League_Championship_Series "1998 American League Championship Series") in six games to the [New York Yankees](/wiki/1998_New_York_Yankees_season "1998 New York Yankees season"), who had come into the postseason with a then-AL record 114 wins in the regular season.[[91]](#cite_note-92)

For the [1999 season](/wiki/1999_Cleveland_Indians_season "1999 Cleveland Indians season"), Cleveland added relief pitcher [Ricardo Rincón](/wiki/Ricardo_Rinc%C3%B3n "Ricardo Rincón") and second baseman [Roberto Alomar](/wiki/Roberto_Alomar "Roberto Alomar"), brother of catcher Sandy Alomar Jr.,[[92]](#cite_note-93) and won the Central Division title for the fifth consecutive year. The team scored 1,009 runs, becoming the first (and to date only) team since the 1950 Boston Red Sox to score more than 1,000 runs in a season. This time, Cleveland did not make it past the first round, losing the [Division Series](/wiki/1999_American_League_Division_Series "1999 American League Division Series") to the [Red Sox](/wiki/1999_Boston_Red_Sox_season "1999 Boston Red Sox season"), despite taking a 2–0 lead in the series. In game three, Indians starter Dave Burba went down with an injury in the 4th inning.[[93]](#cite_note-Burba-94) Four pitchers, including presumed game four starter [Jaret Wright](/wiki/Jaret_Wright "Jaret Wright"), surrendered nine runs in relief. Without a long reliever or emergency starter on the playoff roster, Hargrove started both [Bartolo Colón](/wiki/Bartolo_Col%C3%B3n "Bartolo Colón") and [Charles Nagy](/wiki/Charles_Nagy "Charles Nagy") in games four and five on only three days rest.[[93]](#cite_note-Burba-94) The Indians lost game four 23–7 and game five 12–8.[[94]](#cite_note-95) Four days later, Hargrove was dismissed as manager.[[95]](#cite_note-96)

In 2000, the [Indians](/wiki/2000_Cleveland_Indians_season "2000 Cleveland Indians season") had a 44–42 start, but caught fire after the All Star break and went 46–30 the rest of the way to finish 90–72.[[96]](#cite_note-97) The team had one of the league's best offenses that year and a defense that yielded three gold gloves. However, they ended up five games behind the [Chicago White Sox](/wiki/2000_Chicago_White_Sox_season "2000 Chicago White Sox season") in the Central division and missed the wild card by one game to the [Seattle Mariners](/wiki/2000_Seattle_Mariners_season "2000 Seattle Mariners season"). Mid-season trades brought [Bob Wickman](/wiki/Bob_Wickman "Bob Wickman") and [Jake Westbrook](/wiki/Jake_Westbrook "Jake Westbrook") to Cleveland. After the season, free-agent outfielder [Manny Ramírez](/wiki/Manny_Ram%C3%ADrez "Manny Ramírez") departed for the [Boston Red Sox](/wiki/Boston_Red_Sox "Boston Red Sox").

In 2000, [Larry J. Dolan](/wiki/Larry_Dolan "Larry Dolan") bought the Indians for $320 million from Richard E. Jacobs, who, along with his late brother David, had paid $45 million for the club in 1986. The sale set a record at the time for the sale of a baseball franchise.[[97]](#cite_note-98)

[2001](/wiki/2001_Cleveland_Indians_season "2001 Cleveland Indians season") saw a return to the postseason. After the departures of Ramírez and [Sandy Alomar Jr.](/wiki/Sandy_Alomar_Jr. "Sandy Alomar Jr."), the Indians signed [Ellis Burks](/wiki/Ellis_Burks "Ellis Burks") and former [MVP](/wiki/MLB_Most_Valuable_Player_Award "MLB Most Valuable Player Award") [Juan González](/wiki/Juan_Gonz%C3%A1lez_(baseball) "Juan González (baseball)"), who helped the team win the Central division with a 91–71 record. One of the highlights came on August 5, when the Indians completed the biggest comeback in MLB History. Cleveland rallied to close a 14–2 deficit in the seventh inning to defeat the [Seattle Mariners](/wiki/2001_Seattle_Mariners_season "2001 Seattle Mariners season") 15–14 in 11 innings. The Mariners, who won an MLB record-tying 116 games that season, had a strong bullpen, and Indians manager [Charlie Manuel](/wiki/Charlie_Manuel "Charlie Manuel") had already pulled many of his starters with the game seemingly out of reach.

Seattle and Cleveland met in the [first round of the postseason](/wiki/2001_American_League_Division_Series "2001 American League Division Series"); however, the Mariners won the series 3–2. In the 2001–02 offseason, GM John Hart resigned and his assistant, [Mark Shapiro](/wiki/Mark_Shapiro_(sports_executive) "Mark Shapiro (sports executive)"), took the reins.

### 2002–2010: The Shapiro/Wedge years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cleveland_Guardians&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: 2002–2010: The Shapiro/Wedge years")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/a/ab/Mark_Shapiro.png)](/wiki/File:Mark_Shapiro.png)

[Mark Shapiro](/wiki/Mark_Shapiro_(sports_executive) "Mark Shapiro (sports executive)") – Indians GM from 2001 to 2010, President from 2010 to 2015, and two-time [Sporting News Executive of the Year](/wiki/Sporting_News_Executive_of_the_Year_Award "Sporting News Executive of the Year Award")

#### First "rebuilding of the team"

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cleveland_Guardians&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: First \"rebuilding of the team\"")]

Shapiro moved to rebuild by dealing aging veterans for younger talent. He traded [Roberto Alomar](/wiki/Roberto_Alomar "Roberto Alomar") to the [New York Mets](/wiki/New_York_Mets "New York Mets") for a package that included outfielder [Matt Lawton](/wiki/Matt_Lawton "Matt Lawton") and prospects [Alex Escobar](/wiki/Alex_Escobar "Alex Escobar") and [Billy Traber](/wiki/Billy_Traber "Billy Traber"). When the team fell out of contention in mid-[2002](/wiki/2002_Major_League_Baseball_season "2002 Major League Baseball season"), Shapiro fired manager [Charlie Manuel](/wiki/Charlie_Manuel "Charlie Manuel") and traded pitching ace [Bartolo Colón](/wiki/Bartolo_Col%C3%B3n "Bartolo Colón") for prospects [Brandon Phillips](/wiki/Brandon_Phillips "Brandon Phillips"), [Cliff Lee](/wiki/Cliff_Lee "Cliff Lee"), and [Grady Sizemore](/wiki/Grady_Sizemore "Grady Sizemore"); acquired [Travis Hafner](/wiki/Travis_Hafner "Travis Hafner") from the [Rangers](/wiki/Texas_Rangers_(baseball) "Texas Rangers (baseball)") for [Ryan Drese](/wiki/Ryan_Drese "Ryan Drese") and [Einar Díaz](/wiki/Einar_D%C3%ADaz "Einar Díaz"); and picked up [Coco Crisp](/wiki/Coco_Crisp "Coco Crisp") from the [St. Louis Cardinals](/wiki/St._Louis_Cardinals "St. Louis Cardinals") for aging starter [Chuck Finley](/wiki/Chuck_Finley "Chuck Finley"). [Jim Thome](/wiki/Jim_Thome "Jim Thome") left after the season, going to the Phillies for a larger contract.

Young Indians teams finished far out of contention in 2002 and [2003](/wiki/2003_Major_League_Baseball_season "2003 Major League Baseball season") under new manager [Eric Wedge](/wiki/Eric_Wedge "Eric Wedge"). They posted strong offensive numbers in [2004](/wiki/2004_Major_League_Baseball_season "2004 Major League Baseball season"), but continued to struggle with a bullpen that blew more than 20 saves. A highlight of the season was a 22–0 victory over the [New York Yankees](/wiki/2004_New_York_Yankees_season "2004 New York Yankees season") on August 31, one of the worst defeats suffered by the Yankees in team history.[[98]](#cite_note-99)

In early [2005](/wiki/2005_Major_League_Baseball_season "2005 Major League Baseball season"), the offense got off to a poor start. After a brief July slump, the [Indians](/wiki/2005_Cleveland_Indians_season "2005 Cleveland Indians season") caught fire in August, and cut a 15.5 game deficit in the Central Division down to 1.5 games. However, the season came to an end as the Indians went on to lose six of their last seven games, five of them by one run, missing the playoffs by only two games. Shapiro was named [Executive of the Year](/wiki/Sporting_News_Executive_of_the_Year_Award "Sporting News Executive of the Year Award") in 2005.[[99]](#cite_note-cleveland.com-100) [The next season](/wiki/2006_Major_League_Baseball_season "2006 Major League Baseball season"), the club made several roster changes, while retaining its nucleus of young players. The off-season was highlighted by the acquisition of top prospect [Andy Marte](/wiki/Andy_Marte "Andy Marte") from the [Boston Red Sox](/wiki/Boston_Red_Sox "Boston Red Sox"). The Indians had a solid offensive season, led by career years from [Travis Hafner](/wiki/Travis_Hafner "Travis Hafner") and [Grady Sizemore](/wiki/Grady_Sizemore "Grady Sizemore"). Hafner, despite missing the last month of the season, tied the single season grand slam record of six, which was set in [1987](/wiki/1987_Major_League_Baseball_season "1987 Major League Baseball season") by [Don Mattingly](/wiki/Don_Mattingly "Don Mattingly").[[100]](#cite_note-101) Despite the solid offensive performance, the bullpen struggled with 23 blown saves (a Major League worst), and the Indians finished a disappointing fourth.[[101]](#cite_note-102)

In [2007](/wiki/2007_Major_League_Baseball_season "2007 Major League Baseball season"), Shapiro signed veteran help for the bullpen and outfield in the offseason. Veterans [Aaron Fultz](/wiki/Aaron_Fultz "Aaron Fultz") and [Joe Borowski](/wiki/Joe_Borowski_(baseball) "Joe Borowski (baseball)") joined [Rafael Betancourt](/wiki/Rafael_Betancourt "Rafael Betancourt") in the Indians bullpen.[[102]](#cite_note-MLB2007-103) The Indians improved significantly over the prior year and went into the All-Star break in second place. The team brought back [Kenny Lofton](/wiki/Kenny_Lofton "Kenny Lofton") for his third stint with the team in late July.[[103]](#cite_note-104) The Indians finished with a 96–66 record tied with the Red Sox for best in baseball, their seventh Central Division title in 13 years and their first postseason trip since 2001.[[104]](#cite_note-WP2007-105)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/CC_Sabathia.jpg/250px-CC_Sabathia.jpg)](/wiki/File:CC_Sabathia.jpg)

[CC Sabathia](/wiki/CC_Sabathia "CC Sabathia") won the 2007 AL [Cy Young Award](/wiki/Cy_Young_Award "Cy Young Award") with the Indians.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/Cliff_Lee_Follows_Through.jpg/250px-Cliff_Lee_Follows_Through.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cliff_Lee_Follows_Through.jpg)

Sabathia's teammate [Cliff Lee](/wiki/Cliff_Lee "Cliff Lee") won the AL Cy Young Award in 2008.

The Indians began their playoff run by defeating the [Yankees](/wiki/2007_New_York_Yankees_season "2007 New York Yankees season") in the [ALDS](/wiki/2007_American_League_Division_Series "2007 American League Division Series") three games to one. This series will be most remembered for the swarm of bugs that overtook the field in the later innings of Game Two. They also jumped out to a three-games-to-one lead over the [Red Sox](/wiki/Boston_Red_Sox "Boston Red Sox") in the [ALCS](/wiki/2007_American_League_Championship_Series "2007 American League Championship Series"). The season ended in disappointment when Boston swept the final three games to advance to the [2007 World Series](/wiki/2007_World_Series "2007 World Series").[[104]](#cite_note-WP2007-105)

Despite the loss, Cleveland players took home a number of awards. [Grady Sizemore](/wiki/Grady_Sizemore "Grady Sizemore"), who had a .995 [fielding percentage](/wiki/Fielding_percentage "Fielding percentage") and only two errors in 405 chances, won the [Gold Glove award](/wiki/Rawlings_Gold_Glove_Award "Rawlings Gold Glove Award"), Cleveland's first since 2001.[[105]](#cite_note-106) Indians Pitcher [CC Sabathia](/wiki/CC_Sabathia "CC Sabathia") won the second [Cy Young Award](/wiki/Cy_Young_Award "Cy Young Award") in team history with a 19–7 record, a 3.21 ERA and an MLB-leading 241 [innings pitched](/wiki/Innings_pitched "Innings pitched").[[106]](#cite_note-107) Eric Wedge was awarded the first [Manager of the Year Award](/wiki/Manager_of_the_Year_Award "Manager of the Year Award") in team history.[[107]](#cite_note-108) Shapiro was named to his second [Executive of the Year](/wiki/Sporting_News_Executive_of_the_Year_Award "Sporting News Executive of the Year Award") in 2007.[[99]](#cite_note-cleveland.com-100)

#### Second "rebuilding of the team"

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cleveland_Guardians&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Second \"rebuilding of the team\"")]

The [Indians](/wiki/2008_Cleveland_Indians_season "2008 Cleveland Indians season") struggled during the 2008 season. Injuries to sluggers [Travis Hafner](/wiki/Travis_Hafner "Travis Hafner") and [Victor Martinez](/wiki/Victor_Martinez_(baseball) "Victor Martinez (baseball)"), as well as starting pitchers [Jake Westbrook](/wiki/Jake_Westbrook "Jake Westbrook") and [Fausto Carmona](/wiki/Fausto_Carmona "Fausto Carmona") led to a poor start.[[108]](#cite_note-109) The Indians, falling to last place for a short time in June and July, traded [CC Sabathia](/wiki/CC_Sabathia "CC Sabathia") to the [Milwaukee Brewers](/wiki/Milwaukee_Brewers "Milwaukee Brewers") for prospects [Matt LaPorta](/wiki/Matt_LaPorta "Matt LaPorta"), Rob Bryson, and [Michael Brantley](/wiki/Michael_Brantley "Michael Brantley").[[109]](#cite_note-110) and traded starting third baseman Casey Blake for catching prospect [Carlos Santana](/wiki/Carlos_Santana_(baseball) "Carlos Santana (baseball)").[[110]](#cite_note-111) Pitcher [Cliff Lee](/wiki/Cliff_Lee "Cliff Lee") went 22–3 with an ERA of 2.54 and earned the AL Cy Young Award.[[111]](#cite_note-112) [Grady Sizemore](/wiki/Grady_Sizemore "Grady Sizemore") had a career year, winning a [Gold Glove Award](/wiki/Gold_Glove_Award "Gold Glove Award") and a [Silver Slugger Award](/wiki/Silver_Slugger_Award "Silver Slugger Award"),[[112]](#cite_note-113) and the Indians finished with a record of 81–81.

Prospects for the 2009 season dimmed early when the [Indians](/wiki/2009_Cleveland_Indians_season "2009 Cleveland Indians season") ended May with a record of 22–30. Shapiro made multiple trades: [Cliff Lee](/wiki/Cliff_Lee "Cliff Lee") and [Ben Francisco](/wiki/Ben_Francisco "Ben Francisco") to the [Philadelphia Phillies](/wiki/Philadelphia_Phillies "Philadelphia Phillies") for prospects [Jason Knapp](/wiki/Jason_Knapp_(baseball) "Jason Knapp (baseball)"), [Carlos Carrasco](/wiki/Carlos_Carrasco_(baseball) "Carlos Carrasco (baseball)"), [Jason Donald](/wiki/Jason_Donald_(baseball) "Jason Donald (baseball)") and [Lou Marson](/wiki/Lou_Marson "Lou Marson"); [Victor Martinez](/wiki/V%C3%ADctor_Mart%C3%ADnez_(baseball) "Víctor Martínez (baseball)") to the [Boston Red Sox](/wiki/Boston_Red_Sox "Boston Red Sox") for prospects [Bryan Price](/wiki/Bryan_Price_(pitcher) "Bryan Price (pitcher)"), [Nick Hagadone](/wiki/Nick_Hagadone "Nick Hagadone") and [Justin Masterson](/wiki/Justin_Masterson "Justin Masterson"); [Ryan Garko](/wiki/Ryan_Garko "Ryan Garko") to the [Texas Rangers](/wiki/Texas_Rangers_(baseball) "Texas Rangers (baseball)") for [Scott Barnes](/wiki/Scott_Barnes_(baseball) "Scott Barnes (baseball)"); and [Kelly Shoppach](/wiki/Kelly_Shoppach "Kelly Shoppach") to the [Tampa Bay Rays](/wiki/Tampa_Bay_Rays "Tampa Bay Rays") for [Mitch Talbot](/wiki/Mitch_Talbot "Mitch Talbot"). The Indians finished the season tied for fourth in their division, with a record of 65–97. The team announced on September 30, 2009, that Eric Wedge and all of the team's coaching staff were released at the end of the 2009 season.[[113]](#cite_note-114) [Manny Acta](/wiki/Manny_Acta "Manny Acta") was hired as the team's 40th manager on October 25, 2009.[[114]](#cite_note-115)

On February 18, 2010, it was announced that Shapiro (following the end of the 2010 season) would be promoted to team President, with current President [Paul J. Dolan](/wiki/Paul_Dolan_(baseball) "Paul Dolan (baseball)") becoming the new Chairman/CEO, and longtime Shapiro assistant [Chris Antonetti](/wiki/Chris_Antonetti "Chris Antonetti") filling the GM role.[[115]](#cite_note-116)

### 2011–present: Antonetti/Chernoff/Francona era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cleveland_Guardians&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: 2011–present: Antonetti/Chernoff/Francona era")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/Mike_Chernoff_%28baseball%29_2017-01-27_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Mike_Chernoff_%28baseball%29_2017-01-27_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mike_Chernoff_(baseball)_2017-01-27_(cropped).jpg)

[Mike Chernoff](/wiki/Mike_Chernoff_(baseball) "Mike Chernoff (baseball)"), who has served as Indians/Guardians' general manager since 2015
