[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/Terry_Francona_on_June_26%2C_2013.jpg/250px-Terry_Francona_on_June_26%2C_2013.jpg)](/wiki/File:Terry_Francona_on_June_26,_2013.jpg)

Former manager [Terry Francona](/wiki/Terry_Francona "Terry Francona"), who in his tenure with the Indians/Guardians (2013–2023) was a three-time AL Manager of the Year (2013, 2016, 2022), led the team to the 2016 AL Championship, and is the all-time franchise leader in wins by a manager

On January 18, 2011, longtime popular former first baseman and manager [Mike Hargrove](/wiki/Mike_Hargrove "Mike Hargrove") was brought in as a special adviser. The Indians started the 2011 season strong – going 30–15 in their first 45 games and seven games ahead of the [Detroit Tigers](/wiki/2011_Detroit_Tigers_season "2011 Detroit Tigers season") for first place. Injuries led to a slump where the Indians fell out of first place. Many minor leaguers such as [Jason Kipnis](/wiki/Jason_Kipnis "Jason Kipnis") and [Lonnie Chisenhall](/wiki/Lonnie_Chisenhall "Lonnie Chisenhall") got opportunities to fill in for the injuries.[[116]](#cite_note-117) The biggest news of the season came on July 30 when the Indians traded four prospects for [Colorado Rockies](/wiki/Colorado_Rockies "Colorado Rockies") star pitcher, [Ubaldo Jiménez](/wiki/Ubaldo_Jim%C3%A9nez "Ubaldo Jiménez"). The Indians sent their top two pitchers in the minors, [Alex White](/wiki/Alex_White_(baseball) "Alex White (baseball)") and [Drew Pomeranz](/wiki/Drew_Pomeranz "Drew Pomeranz") along with [Joe Gardner](/w/index.php?title=Joe_Gardner_(baseball)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Joe Gardner (baseball) (page does not exist)") and [Matt McBride](/wiki/Matt_McBride "Matt McBride").[[117]](#cite_note-118) On August 25, the Indians signed the team leader in home runs, [Jim Thome](/wiki/Jim_Thome "Jim Thome") off of waivers.[[118]](#cite_note-119) He made his first appearance in an Indians uniform since he left Cleveland after the 2002 season. To honor Thome, the Indians placed him at his original position, third base, for one pitch against the Minnesota Twins on September 25. It was his first appearance at third base since 1996, and his last for Cleveland.[[119]](#cite_note-120) The Indians finished the season in 2nd place, 15 games behind the division champion Tigers.[[120]](#cite_note-121)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/Corey_Kluber_on_June_27%2C_2013.jpg/250px-Corey_Kluber_on_June_27%2C_2013.jpg)](/wiki/File:Corey_Kluber_on_June_27,_2013.jpg)

[Corey Kluber](/wiki/Corey_Kluber "Corey Kluber"), who is a two-time AL [Cy Young Award](/wiki/Cy_Young_Award "Cy Young Award") winner with the Indians (2014, 2017)

The Indians broke [Progressive Field](/wiki/Progressive_Field "Progressive Field")'s Opening Day attendance record with 43,190 against the [Toronto Blue Jays](/wiki/2012_Toronto_Blue_Jays_season "2012 Toronto Blue Jays season") on April 5, 2012. The game went 16 innings, setting the MLB Opening Day record, and lasted 5 hours and 14 minutes.[[121]](#cite_note-122)

On September 27, 2012, with six games left in the Indians' 2012 season, [Manny Acta](/wiki/Manny_Acta "Manny Acta") was fired; [Sandy Alomar Jr.](/wiki/Sandy_Alomar_Jr. "Sandy Alomar Jr.") was named interim manager for the remainder of the season.[[122]](#cite_note-123) On October 6, the Indians announced that [Terry Francona](/wiki/Terry_Francona "Terry Francona"), who managed the Boston Red Sox to five playoff appearances and two World Series between 2004 and 2011, would take over as manager for 2013.[[123]](#cite_note-foxsports-124)

The Indians entered the 2013 season following an active offseason of dramatic roster turnover. Key acquisitions included free agent 1B/OF [Nick Swisher](/wiki/Nick_Swisher "Nick Swisher") and CF [Michael Bourn](/wiki/Michael_Bourn "Michael Bourn").[[124]](#cite_note-rosenthal-125) The team added prized right-handed pitching prospect [Trevor Bauer](/wiki/Trevor_Bauer "Trevor Bauer"), OF [Drew Stubbs](/wiki/Drew_Stubbs "Drew Stubbs"), and relief pitchers [Bryan Shaw](/wiki/Bryan_Shaw_(baseball) "Bryan Shaw (baseball)") and [Matt Albers](/wiki/Matt_Albers "Matt Albers") in a three-way trade with the [Arizona Diamondbacks](/wiki/Arizona_Diamondbacks "Arizona Diamondbacks") and [Cincinnati Reds](/wiki/Cincinnati_Reds "Cincinnati Reds") that sent RF [Shin-Soo Choo](/wiki/Shin-Soo_Choo "Shin-Soo Choo") to the Reds, and [Tony Sipp](/wiki/Tony_Sipp "Tony Sipp") to the [Arizona Diamondbacks](/wiki/Arizona_Diamondbacks "Arizona Diamondbacks").[[125]](#cite_note-126) Other notable additions included utility man [Mike Avilés](/wiki/Mike_Avil%C3%A9s "Mike Avilés"), catcher [Yan Gomes](/wiki/Yan_Gomes "Yan Gomes"), designated hitter [Jason Giambi](/wiki/Jason_Giambi "Jason Giambi"), and starting pitcher [Scott Kazmir](/wiki/Scott_Kazmir "Scott Kazmir").[[124]](#cite_note-rosenthal-125)[[126]](#cite_note-hoynes-127) The 2013 Indians increased their win total by 24 over 2012 (from 68 to 92), finishing in second place, one game behind Detroit in the Central division, but securing the number one seed in the American League Wild Card Standings. In their first postseason appearance since 2007, Cleveland lost the [2013 American League Wild Card Game](/wiki/2013_American_League_Wild_Card_Game "2013 American League Wild Card Game") 4–0 at home to Tampa Bay. Francona was recognized for the turnaround with the 2013 [American League Manager of the Year](/wiki/American_League_Manager_of_the_Year "American League Manager of the Year") Award.

With an 85–77 record, the 2014 Indians had consecutive winning seasons for the first time since 1999–2001, but they were eliminated from playoff contention during the last week of the season and finished third in the AL Central.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/Shane_Bieber_February_1%2C_2020_%2849488948046%29.jpg/250px-Shane_Bieber_February_1%2C_2020_%2849488948046%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Shane_Bieber_February_1,_2020_(49488948046).jpg)

[Shane Bieber](/wiki/Shane_Bieber "Shane Bieber"), who won the 2020 AL Cy Young Award, giving the team five winners in 14 seasons

In 2015, after struggling through the first half of the season, the Indians finished 81–80 for their third consecutive winning season, which the team had not done since 1999–2001. For the second straight year, the Tribe finished third in the Central and was eliminated from the Wild Card race during the last week of the season. Following the departure of longtime team executive [Mark Shapiro](/wiki/Mark_Shapiro_(sports_executive) "Mark Shapiro (sports executive)") on October 6, the Indians promoted GM [Chris Antonetti](/wiki/Chris_Antonetti "Chris Antonetti") to President of Baseball Operations, assistant general manager [Mike Chernoff](/wiki/Mike_Chernoff_(baseball) "Mike Chernoff (baseball)") to GM, and named [Derek Falvey](/wiki/Derek_Falvey "Derek Falvey") as assistant GM.[[127]](#cite_note-128) Falvey was later hired by the [Minnesota Twins](/wiki/Minnesota_Twins "Minnesota Twins") in 2016, becoming their President of Baseball Operations.

The Indians set what was then a franchise record for longest winning streak when they won their 14th consecutive game, a 2–1 win over the [Toronto Blue Jays](/wiki/2016_Toronto_Blue_Jays_season "2016 Toronto Blue Jays season") in 19 innings on July 1, 2016, at [Rogers Centre](/wiki/Rogers_Centre "Rogers Centre").[[128]](#cite_note-129)[[129]](#cite_note-130) The team clinched the Central Division pennant on September 26, their eighth division title overall and first since 2007, as well as returning to the playoffs for the first time since 2013. They finished the regular season at 94–67, marking their fourth straight winning season, a feat not accomplished since the 1990s and early 2000s.

The Indians began the 2016 postseason by sweeping the [Boston Red Sox](/wiki/2016_Boston_Red_Sox_season "2016 Boston Red Sox season") in the best-of-five [American League Division Series](/wiki/2016_American_League_Division_Series "2016 American League Division Series"), then defeated the Blue Jays in five games in the [2016 American League Championship Series](/wiki/2016_American_League_Championship_Series "2016 American League Championship Series") to claim their sixth American League pennant and advance to the [World Series](/wiki/2016_World_Series "2016 World Series") against the [Chicago Cubs](/wiki/2016_Chicago_Cubs_season "2016 Chicago Cubs season"). It marked the first appearance for the Indians in the World Series since 1997 and first for the Cubs since 1945. The Indians took a 3–1 series lead following a victory in Game 4 at [Wrigley Field](/wiki/Wrigley_Field "Wrigley Field"), but the Cubs rallied to take the final three games and won the series 4 games to 3. The Indians' 2016 success led to Francona winning his second AL Manager of the Year Award with the club.

From August 24 through September 15 during the 2017 season, the Indians set a new American League record by winning 22 games in a row.[[130]](#cite_note-131) On September 28, the Indians won their 100th game of the season, marking only the third time in history the team has reached that milestone. They finished the regular season with 102 wins, second-most in team history (behind 1954's 111 win team). The Indians earned the AL Central title for the second consecutive year, along with home-field advantage throughout the American League playoffs, but they lost the [2017 ALDS](/wiki/2017_ALDS "2017 ALDS") to the [Yankees](/wiki/2017_New_York_Yankees_season "2017 New York Yankees season") 3–2 after being up 2–0.[[131]](#cite_note-132)

In 2018, the Indians won their third consecutive AL Central crown with a 91–71 record, but were swept in the [2018 American League Division Series](/wiki/2018_American_League_Division_Series "2018 American League Division Series") by the [Houston Astros](/wiki/2018_Houston_Astros_season "2018 Houston Astros season"), who outscored Cleveland 21–6. In 2019, despite a two-game improvement, the Indians missed the playoffs as they trailed three games behind the [Tampa Bay Rays](/wiki/2019_Tampa_Bay_Rays_season "2019 Tampa Bay Rays season") for the second AL Wild Card berth. During the 2020 season (shortened to 60 games because of the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic")), the Indians were 35–25, finishing second behind the [Minnesota Twins](/wiki/Minnesota_Twins "Minnesota Twins") in the AL Central, but qualified for the expanded playoffs. In the best-of-three [AL Wild Card Series](/wiki/2020_MLB_playoffs "2020 MLB playoffs"), the Indians were swept by the [New York Yankees](/wiki/2020_New_York_Yankees_season "2020 New York Yankees season"), ending their season.

In February 2025, [Larry J. Dolan](/wiki/Larry_Dolan "Larry Dolan"), who owned the team since the year 2000 and served as the longest owner in the history of the Cleveland franchise, died.[[132]](#cite_note-133) His son [Paul](/wiki/Paul_Dolan_(baseball) "Paul Dolan (baseball)"), who is listed as the team's "Owner/CEO/Chairman," has held ownership duties over the team since 2013, and is also the only of Larry J. Dolan's children who has held major leadership roles over the Guardians.[[133]](#cite_note-dolaninheritance-134)

#### Guardians rebranding

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cleveland_Guardians&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Guardians rebranding")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cd/Stephen_Vogt_%2853479720675%29.jpg/250px-Stephen_Vogt_%2853479720675%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stephen_Vogt_(53479720675).jpg)

Current manager [Stephen Vogt](/wiki/Stephen_Vogt "Stephen Vogt"), who has led the team to two A.L. Central titles, and is a two-time A.L. Manager of the Year (2024 and 2025)

On December 18, 2020, the team announced that the Indians name and logo would be dropped after the 2021 season, later revealing the replacement to be the Guardians.[[134]](#cite_note-135)[[135]](#cite_note-NYTimesDropTeamname-136)[[5]](#cite_note-ClevelandGuardians-6)[[136]](#cite_note-Cleveland_Indians_choose_Guardians-137) In their first season as the Guardians, the team won the 2022 AL Central Division crown, marking the 11th division title in franchise history. In the best-of-three [AL Wild Card Series](/wiki/2022_American_League_Wild_Card_Series "2022 American League Wild Card Series"), the Guardians won the series against the [Tampa Bay Rays](/wiki/2022_Tampa_Bay_Rays_season "2022 Tampa Bay Rays season") 2–0, to advance to the [AL Division Series](/wiki/2022_American_League_Division_Series "2022 American League Division Series"). The Guardians lost the series to the [New York Yankees](/wiki/2022_New_York_Yankees_season "2022 New York Yankees season") 3–2, ending their season. In June 2022, sports investor [David Blitzer](/wiki/David_Blitzer "David Blitzer") bought a 25% stake in the franchise with an option to acquire controlling interest in 2028.[[137]](#cite_note-138)[[138]](#cite_note-139)

Following Francona's retirement at the end of the 2023 season, the Guardians named [Stephen Vogt](/wiki/Stephen_Vogt "Stephen Vogt") as their new manager on November 6, 2023. Vogt would lead the Guardians to a bounce-back season, winning the AL Central with a 92–69 record. The Guardians would beat their AL Central rival the [Detroit Tigers](/wiki/Detroit_Tigers "Detroit Tigers") in the AL Division Series in five games, but would lose to the [New York Yankees](/wiki/New_York_Yankees "New York Yankees") in the AL Pennant in five games.

In 2025, Vogt led the team back from a 15.5 game deficit in July, to overtake Detroit and win the second straight AL Central title under his watch.

## Rivalries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cleveland_Guardians&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Rivalries")]

### Cincinnati Reds

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cleveland_Guardians&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Cincinnati Reds")]

See also: [Ohio Cup](/wiki/Ohio_Cup "Ohio Cup")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fc/New_Ohio_Cup_Trophy.jpg/250px-New_Ohio_Cup_Trophy.jpg)](/wiki/File:New_Ohio_Cup_Trophy.jpg)

The Ohio Cup trophy

The rivalry with fellow [Ohio](/wiki/Ohio "Ohio") team the [Cincinnati Reds](/wiki/Cincinnati_Reds "Cincinnati Reds") is known as the Battle of Ohio or Buckeye Series and features the [Ohio Cup](/wiki/Ohio_Cup "Ohio Cup") trophy for the winner. Prior to 1997, the winner of the cup was determined by an annual [pre-season](/wiki/Spring_training "Spring training") baseball game, played each year at minor-league [Cooper Stadium](/wiki/Cooper_Stadium "Cooper Stadium") in the state capital of [Columbus](/wiki/Columbus,_Ohio "Columbus, Ohio"), and staged just days before the start of each new [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball") season. A total of eight Ohio Cup games were played, with the Guardians winning six of them. It ended with the start of [interleague play](/wiki/Interleague_play "Interleague play") in 1997. The winner of the game each year was awarded the Ohio Cup in postgame ceremonies. The Ohio Cup was a favorite among baseball fans in Columbus, with attendances regularly topping 15,000.

Since 1997, the two teams have played each other as part of the regular season, with the exception of 2002. The Ohio Cup was reintroduced in 2008 and is presented to the team who wins the most games in the series that season. Initially, the teams played one three-game series per season, meeting in Cleveland in 1997 and Cincinnati the following year. The teams have played two series per season against each other since 1999, with the exception of 2002, one at each ballpark. A format change in 2013 made each series two games, except in years when the AL and NL Central divisions meet in interleague play, where it is usually extended to three games per series.[[139]](#cite_note-140) As of June 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Cleveland_Guardians&action=edit), the Guardians lead the series 77–64.[[140]](#cite_note-141)

### Divisional

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cleveland_Guardians&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Divisional")]

#### Detroit Tigers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cleveland_Guardians&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Detroit Tigers")]

The Guardians share a rivalry with the [Detroit Tigers](/wiki/Detroit_Tigers "Detroit Tigers")[[141]](#cite_note-Rivalries_BYB-142) fueled by geographic proximity between the two cities, and elements from the [intense rivalry between Michigan and Ohio State](/wiki/Michigan%E2%80%93Ohio_State_football_rivalry "Michigan–Ohio State football rivalry"). The rivalry with came to a head on August 7, 2013, with the teams first and second in the AL Central standings. Many Tigers fans who made the short trip to Cleveland started a "Let's go Tigers!" chant while the game was tied in the nith inning. Cleveland fans countered with a "Detroit's bankrupt!" chant, in reference to the [city's 2013 bankruptcy](/wiki/Detroit_bankruptcy "Detroit bankruptcy"). Footage of the chants from [SportsTime Ohio](/wiki/Bally_Sports_Great_Lakes "Bally Sports Great Lakes") circulated online, with many baseball fans on [social media](/wiki/Social_media "Social media") criticizing Cleveland fans for the chant due to the circumstances of [Detroit's financial situation](/wiki/Decline_of_Detroit "Decline of Detroit").[[142]](#cite_note-143) The Tigers ended up defeating Cleveland 6–5 in 14 innings.[[143]](#cite_note-144) The two teams have played almost evenly over their 125-year history with Detroit holding a narrow 1,168–1,167 lead. The teams have met twice in the postseason, with Cleveland winning the [2024 AL Division Series](/wiki/2024_American_League_Division_Series "2024 American League Division Series") and Detroit winning the [2025 AL Wild Card Series](/wiki/2025_American_League_Wild_Card_Series "2025 American League Wild Card Series").[[144]](#cite_note-145)

#### Chicago White Sox

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cleveland_Guardians&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Chicago White Sox")]

The [Chicago White Sox](/wiki/Chicago_White_Sox "Chicago White Sox") are another rival, dating back to the 1959 season, when the Sox slipped past the Indians to win the AL pennant. The rivalry intensified when both clubs were moved to the newly created AL Central in 1994. During that season, the two teams challenged for the division title, with the Indians one game back of Chicago when the season ended in August due to the players' strike. During a game in Chicago, the White Sox confiscated [Albert Belle](/wiki/Albert_Belle "Albert Belle")'s [corked bat](/wiki/Corked_bat "Corked bat"), followed by an attempt by Indians pitcher [Jason Grimsley](/wiki/Jason_Grimsley "Jason Grimsley") to crawl through the [Comiskey Park](/wiki/Comiskey_Park_II "Comiskey Park II") clubhouse ceiling to retrieve it. Belle later signed with the White Sox in 1997, adding additional intensity to the rivalry. In 2005, the White Sox led the division by 15 games in July, only to see the Indians trim the lead to a single game late in the season. However, the White Sox swept a three-game series to end the season to win the division by six games; the Sox later won that year's World Series.

On August 5, 2023, Cleveland third baseman [José Ramírez](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Ram%C3%ADrez_(third_baseman) "José Ramírez (third baseman)") and Chicago shortstop [Tim Anderson](/wiki/Tim_Anderson_(baseball) "Tim Anderson (baseball)") instigated a [bench-clearing brawl](/wiki/Bench-clearing_brawl "Bench-clearing brawl") after Anderson applied a tag to Ramírez. Anderson then attempted to punch Ramírez, after which Ramírez wound up knocking Anderson to the ground with a right hook. Anderson and Ramírez were suspended five and two games, respectively, for their roles in the brawl.[[145]](#cite_note-146)[[146]](#cite_note-147)

## Uniforms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cleveland_Guardians&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Uniforms")]

See also: [Major League Baseball uniforms](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball_uniforms "Major League Baseball uniforms")

The official team colors are navy blue, red, and white.[[5]](#cite_note-ClevelandGuardians-6)[[147]](#cite_note-148)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1c/Cleveland_Guardians_wordmark_logo.svg/250px-Cleveland_Guardians_wordmark_logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Cleveland_Guardians_wordmark_logo.svg)

Guardians wordmark logo, featured on the team's standard white home jerseys

### Home

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cleveland_Guardians&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Home")]

The primary home uniform is white with red and navy blue [piping](/wiki/Piping_(sewing) "Piping (sewing)") around each sleeve. Across the front of the jersey in [script font](/wiki/Script_font "Script font") is the word "Guardians" in red with a navy blue outline, with navy blue undershirts, belts, and socks.

The alternate home jersey is red with a navy blue "diamond C" font "Guardians" trimmed in white on the front, and white and navy blue piping on both sleeves, with navy blue undershirts, belts, and socks.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/0/03/Diamond_C_CLE.png/250px-Diamond_C_CLE.png)](/wiki/File:Diamond_C_CLE.png)

Cleveland in "diamond C" font is featured on the team's standard grey road uniform.

In 2024, the team introduced "City Connect" uniforms, primarily (but not exclusively) worn on Friday home dates. The jerseys are blue with red and white stripes going down the sleeve, featuring "CLE" on the front of the jersey and the player names and numbers on the back (all in a white [art deco](/wiki/Art_deco "Art deco") style font), with sandstone colored pants and red socks featuring a logo which was also introduced in 2024 (a "[Guardians of Traffic](/wiki/Guardians_of_Traffic "Guardians of Traffic")" statue holding a baseball bat).[[148]](#cite_note-149)

The standard home cap is red with a navy blue bill, and features a navy blue "diamond C" on the front and is worn with the primary white uniforms. With the alternate red jerseys, the cap is navy blue with a red bill and red "diamond C". The "City Connect" home cap is similar to the alternate cap with the exception of the front section over the bill being white.[[149]](#cite_note-CLEGuardians2025UniChanges-150)

### Road

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cleveland_Guardians&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Road")]

The primary road uniform is gray, with "Cleveland" in navy blue "diamond C" letters, trimmed in red across the front of the jersey, red and navy blue piping around the sleeves, and navy blue undershirts, belts, and socks.

The alternate road jersey is navy blue with a red "diamond C" trimmed in white on the front of the jersey, red and white piping around the sleeves, and navy blue undershirts, belts, and socks.

With either road jersey, the team wears a navy blue cap with a red bill and red "diamond C".

### Universal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cleveland_Guardians&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Universal")]

For all games, the team uses a navy blue [batting helmet](/wiki/Batting_helmet "Batting helmet") with a red "diamond C" on the front.[[149]](#cite_note-CLEGuardians2025UniChanges-150)

All jerseys (sans the "City Connect" version) feature the "winged G" logo on one sleeve, and every jersey has a patch from [Marathon Petroleum](/wiki/Marathon_Petroleum "Marathon Petroleum") – in a sponsorship deal lasting through the 2026 season – on the other. The sleeve featuring the Marathon logo depends on how the player bats – left handed hitters have it on their right sleeve, as that is the arm facing the main TV camera when he bats, and vice versa for right handed batters.[[150]](#cite_note-151)

## Former name and logo controversy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cleveland_Guardians&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Former name and logo controversy")]

Main article: [Cleveland Indians name and logo controversy](/wiki/Cleveland_Indians_name_and_logo_controversy "Cleveland Indians name and logo controversy")

See also: [Native American mascot controversy](/wiki/Native_American_mascot_controversy "Native American mascot controversy")

* [![Logo from 1946 to 1950](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/Logo_of_the_Cleveland_Indians_%281946-1950%29.png/120px-Logo_of_the_Cleveland_Indians_%281946-1950%29.png)](/wiki/File:Logo_of_the_Cleveland_Indians_(1946-1950).png "Logo from 1946 to 1950")

  Logo from 1946 to 1950
* [![Chief Wahoo logo used from 1949 through 2018](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/d/dd/Chief_Wahoo_%28mascot_character%29.svg/120px-Chief_Wahoo_%28mascot_character%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Chief_Wahoo_(mascot_character).svg "Chief Wahoo logo used from 1949 through 2018")

  [Chief Wahoo](/wiki/Chief_Wahoo "Chief Wahoo") logo used from 1949 through 2018
* [!["Block C" logo used secondarily from 2014 until 2019, then as the team's primary logo from 2019 through 2021 – the final three years under the Indians name](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/Cleveland_Indians_primary_logo.svg/120px-Cleveland_Indians_primary_logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Cleveland_Indians_primary_logo.svg "\"Block C\" logo used secondarily from 2014 until 2019, then as the team's primary logo from 2019 through 2021 – the final three years under the Indians name")

  "Block C" logo used secondarily from 2014 until 2019, then as the team's primary logo from 2019 through 2021 – the final three years under the Indians name

The club name and its cartoon logo have been criticized for perpetuating Native American stereotypes. In 1997 and 1998, protesters were arrested after effigies were burned. Charges were dismissed in the 1997 case, and were not filed in the 1998 case. Protesters arrested in the 1998 incident subsequently fought and lost a lawsuit alleging that their [First Amendment](/wiki/First_Amendment_to_the_United_States_Constitution "First Amendment to the United States Constitution") rights had been violated.[[151]](#cite_note-152)[[152]](#cite_note-153)[[153]](#cite_note-OSSC-154)[[154]](#cite_note-155)

[Bud Selig](/wiki/Bud_Selig "Bud Selig") (then–[commissioner of baseball](/wiki/Commissioner_of_Baseball "Commissioner of Baseball")) said in 2014 that he had never received a complaint about the logo. He has heard that there are some protesting against the mascots, but individual teams such as the Indians and [Atlanta Braves](/wiki/Atlanta_Braves "Atlanta Braves"), whose name was also criticized for similar reasons, should make their own decisions.[[155]](#cite_note-156) An organized group consisting of Native Americans, which had protested for many years, protested [Chief Wahoo](/wiki/Chief_Wahoo "Chief Wahoo") on [Opening Day](/wiki/Opening_Day "Opening Day") 2015, noting that this was the 100th anniversary since the team became the Indians. Owner [Paul Dolan](/wiki/Paul_Dolan_(baseball) "Paul Dolan (baseball)"), while stating his respect for the critics, said he mainly heard from fans who wanted to keep Chief Wahoo, and had no plans to change.[[156]](#cite_note-157)

On January 29, 2018, Major League Baseball announced that Chief Wahoo would be removed from the Indians' uniforms for the 2019 season, stating that the logo was no longer appropriate for on-field use.[[157]](#cite_note-158)[[158]](#cite_note-159) The block "C" was promoted to the primary logo; at the time, there were no plans to change the team's name.[[159]](#cite_note-160)

In 2020, [protests](/wiki/George_Floyd_protests "George Floyd protests") over the [murder](/wiki/Murder_of_George_Floyd "Murder of George Floyd") of [George Floyd](/wiki/George_Floyd "George Floyd"), a black man, by a [Minneapolis](/wiki/Minneapolis "Minneapolis") police officer, led the United States into a period of social changes. This made Dolan reconsider use of the Indians name.[[160]](#cite_note-161)[[161]](#cite_note-162) On July 3, 2020, on the heels of the [Washington Redskins](/wiki/Washington_Commanders "Washington Commanders") announcing that they would "undergo a thorough review" of that team's name, the Indians announced that they would "determine the best path forward" regarding the team's name and emphasized the need to "keep improving as an organization on issues of [social justice](/wiki/Social_justice "Social justice")".[[162]](#cite_note-163)

On December 13, 2020, it was reported that the Indians name would be dropped after the 2021 season out of respect for the Native American community.[[135]](#cite_note-NYTimesDropTeamname-136)[[163]](#cite_note-164) It had been hinted by the team that they may move forward without a replacement name (in a similar manner to the Washington Football Team, which used its name for two years until being named the Washington Commanders).[[135]](#cite_note-NYTimesDropTeamname-136)[[164]](#cite_note-165) It was announced via Twitter on July 23, 2021, that the team will be named the Guardians, after the *Guardians of Traffic*, eight large Art Deco statues on the [Hope Memorial Bridge](/wiki/Hope_Memorial_Bridge "Hope Memorial Bridge"), located close to Progressive Field.[[165]](#cite_note-166)

The club, however, found itself amid a trademark dispute with a men's roller derby team called the [Cleveland Guardians](/wiki/Cleveland_Guardians_Roller_Derby "Cleveland Guardians Roller Derby").[[166]](#cite_note-ClevelandGuardiansCopyright-167)[[167]](#cite_note-168)[[168]](#cite_note-169) The Cleveland Guardians roller derby team has competed in the [Men's Roller Derby Association](/wiki/Men%27s_Roller_Derby_Association "Men's Roller Derby Association") since 2016.[[169]](#cite_note-170) In addition, two other entities have attempted to preempt the team's use of the trademark by filing their own registrations with the [U.S. Patent and Trademark Office](/wiki/U.S._Patent_and_Trademark_Office "U.S. Patent and Trademark Office").[[166]](#cite_note-ClevelandGuardiansCopyright-167) The roller derby team filed a federal lawsuit in the [U.S. District Court for the Northern District of Ohio](/wiki/U.S._District_Court_for_the_Northern_District_of_Ohio "U.S. District Court for the Northern District of Ohio") on October 27, 2021, seeking to block the baseball team's name change.[[170]](#cite_note-171)[[171]](#cite_note-172)[[172]](#cite_note-173) On November 16, 2021, the lawsuit was resolved, and both teams were allowed to continue using the Guardians name. The name change from Indians to Guardians became official on November 19, 2021.[[173]](#cite_note-174)[[174]](#cite_note-175)[[5]](#cite_note-ClevelandGuardians-6)[[175]](#cite_note-Bell-176)[[176]](#cite_note-MLB_Advanced_Media-177)[[135]](#cite_note-NYTimesDropTeamname-136)[[136]](#cite_note-Cleveland_Indians_choose_Guardians-137)[*[excessive citations](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#Bundling_citations "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

## Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cleveland_Guardians&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Media")]

Main article: [List of Cleveland Guardians broadcasters](/wiki/List_of_Cleveland_Guardians_broadcasters "List of Cleveland Guardians broadcasters")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/US_Navy_060828-N-1805P-007_Chief_of_Naval_Personnel_Vice_Adm._John_C._Harvey_Jr._chats_with_broadcasters_from_Cleveland_radio_station_WTAM_1100_AM_during_a_Major_League_Baseball_game_at_Jacobs_Field_between_the_Cleveland_Indian.jpg/250px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:US_Navy_060828-N-1805P-007_Chief_of_Naval_Personnel_Vice_Adm._John_C._Harvey_Jr._chats_with_broadcasters_from_Cleveland_radio_station_WTAM_1100_AM_during_a_Major_League_Baseball_game_at_Jacobs_Field_between_the_Cleveland_Indian.jpg)

Guardians TV announcer [Matt Underwood](/wiki/Matt_Underwood "Matt Underwood") (seated, center) and longtime lead radio announcer [Tom Hamilton](/wiki/Tom_Hamilton_(broadcaster) "Tom Hamilton (broadcaster)") (right)

### Radio

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cleveland_Guardians&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Radio")]

Cleveland radio stations [WTAM](/wiki/WTAM "WTAM") (1100 [AM](/wiki/AM_broadcasting "AM broadcasting") and 106.9 [FM](/wiki/FM_broadcasting "FM broadcasting")) and [WMMS](/wiki/WMMS "WMMS") (100.7 FM), both owned by [iHeartMedia](/wiki/IHeartMedia "IHeartMedia"), serve as flagship stations for the [Cleveland Guardians Radio Network](/wiki/Cleveland_Guardians_Radio_Network "Cleveland Guardians Radio Network").[[177]](#cite_note-radio-178) [Ford C. Frick Award](/wiki/Ford_C._Frick_Award "Ford C. Frick Award") winner [Tom Hamilton](/wiki/Tom_Hamilton_(broadcaster) "Tom Hamilton (broadcaster)") and [Jim Rosenhaus](/wiki/Jim_Rosenhaus "Jim Rosenhaus") comprise the announcing team and call all games on-site.[[178]](#cite_note-broadcasters-179)

Fellow sister station [WARF](/wiki/WARF "WARF") (1350 AM) - while primarily an English language station - serves as the team's Spanish flagship, airing broadcasts of home games. Rafa Hernández-Brito serves as the primary Spanish announcer, alongside analyst and former Indian [Carlos Baerga](/wiki/Carlos_Baerga "Carlos Baerga").[[179]](#cite_note-180)

### TV

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cleveland_Guardians&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: TV")]

Regular season games not broadcast by MLB's national TV partners are shown exclusively on Guardians TV, operated by [MLB Local Media](/wiki/MLB_Local_Media "MLB Local Media"), which launched on February 11, 2025.[[180]](#cite_note-181) Cleveland [NBC](/wiki/NBC "NBC") affiliate [WKYC](/wiki/WKYC "WKYC") channel 3 simulcasts select games, plus airs a weekly team centered sportscast titled *Guardians Tonight* Sunday nights.[[181]](#cite_note-t536-182)

Lead announcer [Matt Underwood](/wiki/Matt_Underwood "Matt Underwood"), analyst and former Indians [Gold Glove](/wiki/Gold_Glove "Gold Glove")-winning centerfielder [Rick Manning](/wiki/Rick_Manning "Rick Manning"), and field reporter Andre Knott form the TV broadcast team, with [Al Pawlowski](/wiki/Al_Pawlowski "Al Pawlowski") hosting the pre- and postgame shows, alongside former Indian [Chris Gimenez](/wiki/Chris_Gimenez "Chris Gimenez") serving as analyst, with various other former Indians/Guardians making guest appearances. Guardians TV airs on cable systems throughout [Northeast Ohio](/wiki/Northeast_Ohio "Northeast Ohio"), as well as functioning as a [streaming service](/wiki/Streaming_service "Streaming service").[[182]](#cite_note-z8262-183)

### Past announcers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cleveland_Guardians&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Past announcers")]

Notable former broadcasters include Tom Manning, [Jack Graney](/wiki/Jack_Graney "Jack Graney") (the first ex-baseball player to become a play-by-play announcer), [Ken Coleman](/wiki/Ken_Coleman "Ken Coleman"), [Joe Castiglione](/wiki/Joe_Castiglione "Joe Castiglione"), [Van Patrick](/wiki/Van_Patrick "Van Patrick"), [Nev Chandler](/wiki/Nev_Chandler "Nev Chandler"), [Bruce Drennan](/wiki/Bruce_Drennan "Bruce Drennan"), [Jim "Mudcat" Grant](/wiki/Mudcat_Grant "Mudcat Grant"), [Rocky Colavito](/wiki/Rocky_Colavito "Rocky Colavito"), [Dan Coughlin](/wiki/Dan_Coughlin_(sportscaster) "Dan Coughlin (sportscaster)"), and [Jim Donovan](/wiki/Jim_Donovan_(sportscaster) "Jim Donovan (sportscaster)").

Previous broadcasters who have had lengthy tenures with the team include [Joe Tait](/wiki/Joe_Tait "Joe Tait") (15 seasons between TV and radio), [Jack Corrigan](/wiki/Jack_Corrigan_(radio) "Jack Corrigan (radio)") (18 seasons on TV), [Ford C. Frick Award](/wiki/Ford_C._Frick_Award "Ford C. Frick Award") winner [Jimmy Dudley](/wiki/Jimmy_Dudley "Jimmy Dudley") (19 seasons on radio), [Mike Hegan](/wiki/Mike_Hegan "Mike Hegan") (23 seasons between TV and radio), and [Herb Score](/wiki/Herb_Score "Herb Score") (34 seasons between TV and radio).[[183]](#cite_note-184)

## Popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cleveland_Guardians&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Popular culture")]

Under the Cleveland Indians name, the team has been featured in several films, including:

* *[The Kid from Cleveland](/wiki/The_Kid_from_Cleveland "The Kid from Cleveland")* – a 1949 film featuring then-owner [Bill Veeck](/wiki/Bill_Veeck "Bill Veeck") and numerous players from the team (coming off winning the [1948 World Series](/wiki/1948_World_Series "1948 World Series")).
* *[Major League](/wiki/Major_League_(film) "Major League (film)")* – a 1989 film centered around a fictionalized version of the Indians.
* *[Major League II](/wiki/Major_League_II "Major League II")* – a 1994 sequel to the 1989 original.

### Guardians Hall of Fame

Further information: [Cleveland Guardians award winners and league leaders § Cleveland Guardians Hall of Fame](/wiki/Cleveland_Guardians_award_winners_and_league_leaders#Cleveland_Guardians_Hall_of_Fame "Cleveland Guardians award winners and league leaders")

### Statues

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cleveland_Guardians&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: Statues")]

Numerous Naps/Indians players have had statues made in their honor:

#### In and around Progressive Field

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cleveland_Guardians&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: In and around Progressive Field")]

* [Bob Feller](/wiki/Bob_Feller "Bob Feller") (team all-time leader in wins and strikeouts by a pitcher, [1948 World Series](/wiki/1948_World_Series "1948 World Series") Champion, eight-time All-Star) – since 1994\*
* [Jim Thome](/wiki/Jim_Thome "Jim Thome") (team all-time leader in home runs and walks by a hitter, three-time All-Star with the Indians) – since 2014\*
* [Larry Doby](/wiki/Larry_Doby "Larry Doby") (First black player in the American League, [1948 World Series](/wiki/1948_World_Series "1948 World Series") Champion, seven-time All-Star) – since 2015\*
* [Frank Robinson](/wiki/Frank_Robinson "Frank Robinson") (Became first black manager in MLB history when he served as player/manager from 1975 to 1977) – since 2017
* [Lou Boudreau](/wiki/Lou_Boudreau "Lou Boudreau") (1948 AL MVP, [1948 World Series](/wiki/1948_World_Series "1948 World Series") Champion as player/manager, eight-time All-Star) – since 2017\*[[184]](#cite_note-185)

#### In and around Cleveland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cleveland_Guardians&action=edit&section=48 "Edit section: In and around Cleveland")]

* Hall of Fame outfielder [Elmer Flick](/wiki/Elmer_Flick "Elmer Flick") has a statue in his hometown of [Bedford, Ohio](/wiki/Bedford,_Ohio "Bedford, Ohio"), a nearby suburb of Cleveland – since 2013\*
* Former outfielder [Luke Easter](/wiki/Luke_Easter_(baseball) "Luke Easter (baseball)") has a statue outside of his namesake park on the east side of Cleveland – since 1980 (when the park was renamed in Easter's honor following his murder)[[185]](#cite_note-186)
* Five-time All-Star (with the Indians) outfielder [Rocky Colavito](/wiki/Rocky_Colavito "Rocky Colavito") has a statue in Cleveland's [Little Italy](/wiki/Little_Italy,_Cleveland "Little Italy, Cleveland") neighborhood – since August 10, 2021.[[186]](#cite_note-187)[[187]](#cite_note-188)

(\*) – Inducted into the [Baseball Hall of Fame](/wiki/Baseball_Hall_of_Fame "Baseball Hall of Fame") as an Indian/Nap.

### Murals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cleveland_Guardians&action=edit&section=49 "Edit section: Murals")]

In July 2022, in honor of the 75th anniversary of [Larry Doby](/wiki/Larry_Doby "Larry Doby") becoming the AL's first black player, a mural was added to the exterior of Progressive Field, honoring players who were viewed as barrier breakers that played for the Indians/Guardians. The mural features Doby, [Frank Robinson](/wiki/Frank_Robinson "Frank Robinson"), and [Satchel Paige](/wiki/Satchel_Paige "Satchel Paige").[[188]](#cite_note-189)

### Streets

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cleveland_Guardians&action=edit&section=50 "Edit section: Streets")]

* A portion of Eagle Avenue near Progressive Field was renamed "Larry Doby Way" in 2012.[[189]](#cite_note-190)
* Street where his namesake baseball field is located in Cleveland renamed "José Ramírez Way" in 2025[[190]](#cite_note-191)

### Parks and fields

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cleveland_Guardians&action=edit&section=51 "Edit section: Parks and fields")]

A number of parks and newly built and renovated youth baseball fields in Cleveland have been named after former and current Indians/Guardians players, including:

* Luke Easter Park – named for Easter in 1980 following his murder[[191]](#cite_note-192)
* Jim Thome All-Star Complex – 2019[[192]](#cite_note-193)
* CC Sabathia Field at Luke Easter Park – 2021[[193]](#cite_note-194)
* José Ramírez Field at Clark Field– 2023[[194]](#cite_note-195)

### Season records

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cleveland_Guardians&action=edit&section=53 "Edit section: Season records")]

* Highest batting average: .408, [Joe Jackson](/wiki/Shoeless_Joe_Jackson "Shoeless Joe Jackson") (1911)
* Most games: 163, [Leon Wagner](/wiki/Leon_Wagner "Leon Wagner") (1964)
* Most runs: 140, [Earl Averill](/wiki/Earl_Averill "Earl Averill") (1930)
* Highest slugging %: .714, [Albert Belle](/wiki/Albert_Belle "Albert Belle") (1994)
* Most doubles: 64, [George Burns](/wiki/George_Burns_(first_baseman) "George Burns (first baseman)") (1926)
* Most triples: 26, [Joe Jackson](/wiki/Shoeless_Joe_Jackson "Shoeless Joe Jackson") (1912)
* Most home runs: 52, [Jim Thome](/wiki/Jim_Thome "Jim Thome") (2002)
* Most RBIs: 165, [Manny Ramírez](/wiki/Manny_Ram%C3%ADrez "Manny Ramírez") (1999)
* Most stolen bases: 75, [Kenny Lofton](/wiki/Kenny_Lofton "Kenny Lofton") (1996)
* Most wins: 31, [Jim Bagby, Sr.](/wiki/Jim_Bagby,_Sr. "Jim Bagby, Sr.") (1920)
* Lowest ERA: 1.16, [Addie Joss](/wiki/Addie_Joss "Addie Joss") (1908)
* Strikeouts: 348, [Bob Feller](/wiki/Bob_Feller "Bob Feller") (1946)
* Complete games: 36, [Bob Feller](/wiki/Bob_Feller "Bob Feller") (1946)
* Saves: 47, [Emmanuel Clase](/wiki/Emmanuel_Clase "Emmanuel Clase") (2024)
* Longest win streak: 22 games (2017)
